question: You speak of being 'hedged round about on all sides by Thee'—how did that sense of divine constraint feel to you at the time, and why did it not immediately bring you to full surrender?
answer:
Even as a child I had learned to barter my soul for the applause of men, and in youth I wore my intellect like armor, believing wisdom could be seized rather than received. Yet when Thy words took root in me—sharp and unyielding—I found myself encircled not by chains of iron, but by the living wall of Thy presence. To be hedged about by Thee was not imprisonment, but exposure: every flight path blocked, every evasion seen through, every excuse dissolved before it reached my lips. I could no longer wander in darkness pretending to seek light; Thou hadst turned the very world into a mirror, and I stood face-to-face with my own unrest.

But O Lord, though I saw Thee—dimly as through a veil—and though I knew that all substance flowed from Thy incorruptible Being, yet my will trembled at the threshold. My affections were still entangled in the old loves: the sweetness of vanity, the warmth of lustful embraces, the pride that whispered *I am not yet ready*. I desired Thee as one longs for distant beauty seen on a hilltop—admired, adored, yet left unapproached. To surrender meant death: death to ambition’s crown, death to pleasure’s murmurs, death to the self I had so carefully constructed.

And so I shrank from the strait gate. Not because I doubted Thy power or Thy truth—for Thou hadst already conquered me in secret—but because love demands everything, and my heart still clutched at its idols. Like one standing on the edge of healing fire trembling to enter, knowing it will burn away all disease but also all comfort: such was my state. Thou didst hold me fast not to torment me but to break me—gently tearing apart what needed dying so that what could not die might live at last in Thee.

question: You mention that you had ceased to doubt the existence of incorruptible substance—what specifically about my eternal nature convinced you, and why did that certainty not resolve your moral struggles?
answer:
It was not through seeing, but through being seen, O Lord, that my soul first trembled before the certainty of Thee. I had long chased after truth as though it were a thing to be grasped by the hands or cornered by argument, like a wild beast I might subdue with the nets of rhetoric and learning. Yet in the silence between thoughts—when memory turned back not to events, but to desires—I found a restlessness too deep for words, a hunger that no pleasure ever filled and no philosophy could explain. That very unrest became my teacher.

When I beheld within my own mind the unchanging order of numbers and truth—that one is always one, that justice is better than injustice even when punished—I could no longer doubt that there existed some eternal substance, immutable and whole. My intellect bowed before necessity itself: if change exists, there must be something unchanged; if falsehood can be known, there must be Truth itself by which it is judged. Thus did Thy invisible things become visible through the things that are made—even within me.

Yet knowledge of Thee as Creator did not free me from created pleasures. The body’s weight still clung to my soul like damp clay to limbs struggling upward from the mire. I knew the sun was real long before I dared walk into its light; so too I confessed an incorruptible Good, while loving lesser goods with disordered love. The will is not changed because the mind sees clearly—it remains twisted by habit, weakened by indulgence, torn in itself.

I loved Thee at a distance, O Lord, like one who admires a mountain from afar but fears the climb. To know that Thou art—that Thou art pure Being—did not heal my loves; only grace could bend them back toward Thee. Wisdom said “Come,” but delight in sin still whispered “Stay.” And so I stood trembling at the threshold of conversion: certain of God’s eternity, yet uncertain whether I would surrender time itself to Him.

question: Why did you choose Simplicianus in particular to seek guidance from, and what was it about his life that made you trust his spiritual wisdom?
answer:
O Lord, Thou didst incline my steps toward Simplicianus, not by chance nor mere human counsel, but by the hidden hand of Thy mercy guiding my confusion. I sought one who had not only heard of Thee but had been consumed by Thee over the course of a lifetime—whose very age was a testimony, not to passing years, but to enduring faithfulness. Many spoke well of him, yet it was not their praise that drew me; rather, it was the quiet radiance of grace upon his countenance, the kind that no rhetoric can feign and no philosophy manufacture. He had walked with Thee from youth to old age, and in his bearing I perceived a man unshaken by the storms that still tossed me between desire and duty.

I myself was torn—drawn to Thy beauty, yet still clinging to the chains I dared not cast off. The Way lay before me, narrow and bright, but my affections were heavy with the weight of habit. I needed not a teacher of doctrines only, but a witness of transformation; one whose life could say more than his words. And in Simplicianus I saw such a man: gentle in speech, firm in holiness, seasoned by decades of surrender. His years were not merely long—they were deep, carved through by prayer and trial and unwavering devotion.

How could I doubt that one who had spent so long abiding in Thee might know the path to Thee? His very existence whispered hope: if grace could shape such constancy in him, might it not also reach even me—the restless wanderer caught between lust and longing? In seeking him, I sought none other than an echo of Thy voice calling me home.

question: You say your heart had to be purged from 'the old leaven'—what did you mean by that phrase, and what lingering attachments were still holding you back?
answer:
I was still drunk on the wine of my own will, though I had begun to hear the call to sobriety.  

When I spoke of being purged from "the old leaven," O Lord, I meant that corruption which ferments within the soul—pride, lust, and the love of temporal honors—that sour leaven which puffs up but nourishes nothing eternal. It was not that I disbelieved Thee; no, I had tasted Thy truth in the Scriptures, felt Thy presence in the trembling depths of my conscience. But belief alone did not free me from the chains of habit. The body groaned under its old appetites; the mind still dallied with ambition, with the applause of men, with words well-spoken for fame rather than for love of wisdom.  

I desired chastity—yes—and cried out to Thee for it, yet secretly whispered: *not yet*. Not while youth still burned in my veins. Not while friends looked up to me as one who mastered rhetoric, not one mastered by God. Not while a woman’s embrace could quiet the storm inside me more swiftly than prayer—though it left me emptier afterward than silence itself.  

And ambition! O Lord, how it clung like sweat to my skin—even when I turned toward Thee, part of me wanted holiness as another crown to wear before men. To be known as Augustine the convert! Augustine the ascetic! Even virtue became a stage if I stood near enough to an audience.  

This was the old leaven: not heresy in doctrine, but rot in desire. A heart torn between two loves—the world and Thee—and long unable to believe that Thou couldst satisfy its restlessness entirely. I knew that Thou wert real; what I doubted daily was whether Thou wert enough.

But Thou didst not let go. Though I delayed surrendering all things—especially myself—Thou hedged me in with providence until flight was no longer possible and only kneeling remained.

Then came Simplicianus—not because he would solve my hesitation with argument alone (though his words were seasoned wisdom), but because he embodied what grace could do: a man who had walked this path before me and found not death but life on its narrow way.

The old leaven dies slowly—even after truth is seen—but die it must if bread is to rise unto Thee.

question: Though the Way pleased you, you 'shrunk from going through its straitness'—what about the demands of Christian life frightened or repelled you at that moment?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst draw me not by force but by the secret magnetism of hidden beauty, it was not the yoke that frightened me, but the rending—how the very self I loved had to be torn asunder before it could be remade. The Way pleased me indeed, for I saw in Christ a wisdom beyond the schools of rhetoric, a love stronger than death; yet when I drew near, my flesh trembled. Not because Thy commandments were cruel or unjust—nay, they were light, and life itself—but because they demanded all. All my concupiscence, all my pride in speech and in knowledge, all the quiet pleasures I hoarded like stolen pears in the night: these were not to be reformed but abandoned.

I shrank from the narrowness not as one shrinking from truth, but as a man long chained in darkness shrinks from daylight—knowing it is health, yet fearing how it will lay bare every wound and every shame. To follow Thee meant no half-measures: no more saying "Give me chastity" with a sly smile—"but not yet." That "not yet" had been my cradle and my prison. Ambition still whispered in one ear; lust sang lullabies in the other; and philosophy gave me excuses for delay. The strait gate required silence from them all—and silence terrified me.

For what would I be without those voices? A man stripped bare before God—no titles, no triumphs in debate, no secret sins to warm myself by. Only Thou remained. And though my bones thirsted for Thee more than drought-stricken land for rain, my will wavered at the brink. It is one thing to admire virtue in discourse; another to cast down every imagination unto obedience—to surrender not just belief but breath itself into Thy hands.

And yet Thou didst not let go. Thou didst hedge me about with books read at random, with friends who prayed when I only mocked myself inwardly, with a child’s voice chanting *Tolle lege*, until at last I could flee no longer—not even from myself.

question: You said that an older man devoted to God seemed likely to have gained much experience—was this man Ambrose, and why did you place such trust in his judgment?
answer:
In the fever of my youth, when I ran after the siren songs of rhetoric and renown, I believed wisdom could be measured in applause and adorned with eloquence; but when at last my soul grew weary of those hollow triumphs, I turned my eyes to men who had forsaken the stage of the world not for silence, but for prayer — and among them none shone with such quiet fire as Ambrose.

He was not merely old in years, though time had indeed graced him with gray hairs and a face carved by vigilance; no, it was that he had grown deep in God, as a tree whose roots drink from hidden streams. When I came to him — restless still, though no longer chasing honors — I saw in his life no fracture between word and will: what he preached at the altar, he breathed in solitude. Others spoke truth; he lived it. And that gave weight to his silence as much as to his speech.

I placed trust in him because he did not dazzle me with novelty, but dismantled error with patience. He did not yield to the winds of opinion; even when emperors threatened, he stood fast before the altar like a stone in a rushing flood. And yet — O Lord, how sweetly Thou didst ordain it! — he opened Scripture to me not as a fortress to be defended, but as a land to be entered, where every furrow bore seed of mystery and grace.

Though I sought from him counsel on how best to walk in Thy ways — torn between marriage’s comfort and chastity’s crown — it was not finally his experience alone that moved me; it was that I beheld in him a soul wholly given over to Thee. He had chosen the better part, not by nature, but by daily surrender. And so I thought: if this man has dwelt so long beneath Thy shadow, surely he knows the path through the wilderness.

Yet even then my heart wavered: *Give me continence,* I groaned within myself, *but not yet.*

question: When you referred to the church being full and people walking different ways, were you struggling with choosing between monastic life and marriage, and why did that burden weigh so heavily on you?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my breast with the dart of Thy love while I wandered in the thicket of vanity and the shadow of pride—when I saw the church full, it was not order I beheld, but a kind of holy confusion, as though many feet trod one threshold yet each path seemed carved by different longing. I was not a boy chasing honors now, nor a youth drunk on words and applause; no, I had tasted Thee, and all else turned to ash. Yet still my flesh trembled under the old chains.

Yes, I stood at that crossroads: the narrow way of continence, wholly given to Thee like Paul who wished all men were as he was; or the broader path of marriage, which Thou didst not forbid but which held me back from full surrender. And why this burden? Because my heart had been awakened. To know Thee—even briefly—is to be made restless forever after. To have tasted the sweetness of Thy house and then return to the marketplace is torture. 

I loved a woman still—not with the wild fire of my youth, but with a clinging that would not let go. And though marriage is lawful, even blessed in its own place, for me it felt like settling into exile when the homeland was in sight. The Apostle urged singleness for undivided devotion; I longed for that freedom—but lacked the strength. So I chose what seemed more bearable for my weakness, yet found it unbearable in another way: for now I desired Thee wholly while still bound by earthly ties that pulled me back into distraction.

Thou hadst shown me eunuchs for the kingdom’s sake—men who gave up even what is good for the sake of what is highest. And Thou didst say: *Let him who can receive it, receive it.* O Lord, I could not—but neither could I be content without Thee. This was my torment: to see the summit bathed in light and feel my feet sinking in mud too dear to abandon.

question: You mentioned being enthralled with the love of woman—was this about your concubine in Carthage, and why couldn’t you release her despite your spiritual desires?
answer:
O Lord, how deeply I was chained, not by her who shared my bed, but by the lust that bound my soul to the flesh, though she was faithful to me and bore me a son—yet still, I called her not wife, but concubine, as if custom could hide the truth from Thee.  

Yes, it was she—the woman of Carthage, bound to me by no sacrament but by the fever of habit and desire. For years I had said: *“Give me chastity and continence, but not yet,”* and so I delayed surrendering even what seemed small in the eyes of men but heavy in Thine. She was no harlot; she wronged no one; she loved me truly. And yet, because I loved her not for Thee but for myself—with a love swollen with possession and pleasure—I could not let her go without sorrow tearing my heart like a widow’s cry at the grave.

The world called it virtue to keep one woman instead of many; but Thou, O Light that pierces shadows, showed me it was only restraint without purity. When at last they tore her from me—because marriage to another was deemed more seemly for my rising station—I groaned like a wounded man flayed alive. My soul wept tears I had never shed for sin. Was this love? No—this was idolatry veiled in loyalty.  

I could not release her because I feared loneliness more than disobedience; because ambition whispered that holiness might wait; because even when I desired Thee above all things, I still wanted *something* of this world to cling to—a hand in the dark, a voice in the night. But Thou wouldst have none of it. Thou wouldst be all or nothing.

And at last—after long tossing upon waves of doubt and desire—I cast off even that half-life of compromise. She returned to Africa and vowed perpetual chastity in Thy name; and I—broken, emptied—began at last to learn what it means for love to be free: not bent on having, but on giving; not rooted in appetite, but anchored in Thee.

question: You called marriage the 'more indulgent place' because you felt weak—did you later regret choosing it over celibacy, and how did that conflict shape your view of human frailty?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my breast with the darts of longing before ever I knew the wound was Yours, I confess that the path I chose—marriage, as a refuge from lust—was not chosen for love of Thee, but for fear of myself.  

I called it the "more indulgent place" because my will was soft, my bones weak with desire, and my soul still drunk with the noise of the world. I could not bear the austerity of continence, though I admired it; I envied those who had plucked out their passions by the root, while I watered mine with excuses. The Apostle bade me fly higher, and I—I crawled into the shelter of lawful affection, thinking it enough to be chaste within marriage when Thou hadst offered me wings to soar above all chains.  

Did I regret it? Not as one regrets a crime—but as a soldier regrets he did not charge when victory lay open. There was no sin in marriage itself; Thou hast hallowed it like water into wine. But in me there was failure—a failure of trust. I doubted whether Thy grace could sustain me in harder service. And so I settled for peace when Thou invited me to warfare under Thy banner.  

This conflict—the tugging between flesh and spirit—taught me more than any treatise on human frailty ever could. For man is not broken only in his sins; he is broken even in his virtues when they spring from fear rather than faith. How many times did I bow at prayer with a heart divided—loving Thee truly, yet clinging to comfort as if it were salvation? How often did I say to myself: "Later… after this desire is calmed," knowing full well that *later* was a lie wrapped in time?  

And yet—Thou didst use even this weakness for wisdom’s sake. In my very failure to rise above nature, I came to know its weight—the weight of habit, of concupiscence whispering sweetly in the dark hours; the way custom binds us before we notice our hands are tied. From this frailty grew mercy in me: for if *I*, who sought Thee earnestly, stumbled so often just by walking beside one woman—what then shall we say of those who walk through life burdened with greater sins and lesser light?  

Therefore, O God, from that half-chosen life Thou drew forth full teaching: that grace does not despise our stammering steps; that even our retreats become pilgrimage when we turn always toward Thee; and that the heart which mourns its own indulgence may yet be purer than the one boasting of its austerity without love.

question: When you quoted Jesus about eunuchs who made themselves eunuchs for heaven’s sake, why did you feel unable to receive that calling, and what did that reveal to you about your own desires?
answer:
O Lord, how deeply I felt the chasm between what I knew to be good and what my will could bring itself to embrace — not from lack of seeing, but from the weight of loving otherwise. That saying of Christ pierced me like a sword: *There are eunuchs who have made themselves eunuchs for the kingdom of heaven’s sake*. I heard it not as a command laid upon all, but as a light held up in the darkness, showing a path some are given grace to walk — a path of perfect self-giving, where even the deepest natural affections are surrendered at Thy altar.

But when I turned that light inward, upon the chambers of my heart where secret desires still stirred, I found myself trembling. Not that I despised marriage — Thou hast blessed it, O God — but because I saw that my soul was still bound by lust’s soft chains, not with violence, but with sweetness. My will was divided: one part yearning for Thee alone, another clinging to the warmth of woman’s presence, afraid of solitude, afraid of change. I did not wish to remain chained; yet neither could I will to break free.

And so I chose marriage — not because it was highest, but because it was bearable; not because it satisfied my soul, but because it calmed my flesh. Yet even in that choice there was no peace. For though Thou hadst begun to draw me upward out of the mire of worldly ambition and vain delights, Thou hadst not yet delivered me from the tyranny of habit and desire. I wanted Thee to command what Thou already willed; yet delayed obedience while pleading excuse.

That word about eunuchs revealed not merely a way of life too hard for me — it revealed how little I loved Thee above all else. Had my love been whole, had my delight been solely in Thy beauty as it should have been, then even this sacrifice would have tasted sweet. But instead it seemed harsh and strange — not because it was so in truth, but because my heart was so disordered.

I desired Thee fervently in moments; yet lived against Thee daily. And this was my misery: that though I ran toward Thee with tears and longing prayers on my lips , my feet still walked among shadows and pleasures that held me back.

question: You said you had surmounted the vanity of those who could not find God from His creation—what specific experiences led you to move beyond that ignorance and recognize the Creator?
answer:
O Lord, Thou didst catch me not in the splendor of thunder or the flashing of lightning, but in the stillness behind my own restless thoughts, where memory unrolled its vast chambers like an endless night. I had wandered through them all—the corridors of rhetoric, the vaults of lust, the dimly lit halls of ambition—each one echoing with the hollow footsteps of a soul seeking itself. And yet it was within this very depth of memory, where I stored not only past pleasures but also their bitterness, that Thou didst begin to speak.

For how could I see Thee in creation if I had not first seen Thee in the broken mirror of my own soul? The sun rose and I admired not its light but my power to name it; the stars blazed and I measured them with pride rather than wonder. Vain indeed was I who thought himself wise among men, for I read Plato and Plotinus as if they were keys to Thee, O God, when they were but shadows pointing beyond themselves. But when my friend Alypius listened with me to Ambrose preach and we marveled at how Scripture could be opened like a scroll long sealed—then a door stirred in me.

Still, knowledge was not surrender. I knew what was good—Thou hadst made it clear—and yet I loved delay. "Give me chastity," I groaned in secret, "but not yet." The weight of continence terrified me because it meant death to desire as I knew it. Yet Thou didst press closer through disordered loves: through stolen fruit tasted only for the thrill of sinning; through women whose names even now shame me to recall; through books that lifted my mind but left my will bound.

And then came the garden—O unexpected moment!—when laughter from a child’s voice shattered reason’s last defense: *Tolle, lege.* Take up and read. Not philosophy. Not poetry. A letter from Paul that pierced like a sword: *Not in reveling and drunkenness… but put on the Lord Jesus Christ.* Then every evasion collapsed. Then tears gushed forth—not from sorrow alone, but from relief that resistance had failed.

It was not logic alone that led me out of vanity, O Lord; nor beauty merely glimpsed in created things; but Thy mercy pressing inward until even my will could no longer pretend ignorance. Creation whispered everywhere: “He made us,” yet only grace made me hear.

question: When you quoted Christ about eunuchs who made themselves eunuchs for the kingdom, why did you hesitate to fully embrace that radical calling yourself?
answer:
O Lord, who knowest the secret inclinations of the heart, how long I stood trembling on the edge of surrender, drawn by Thy beauty and repelled by the cost! I had read Thy words, spoken in divine simplicity: *There are eunuchs who have made themselves eunuchs for the kingdom of heaven’s sake.* And Thou didst add, *He that can receive it, let him receive it*—as if to say, This is not for all, but for those whom grace unbinds from the chains of desire.  

I understood the meaning well enough: not bodily castration, but the severing of all self-will, the cutting away of every attachment that competes with Thee for dominion over the soul. To live as one dead to lust, to fame, to possession—not out of nature’s defect, but out of love’s excess. And yet within me raged a war: my will shrank back. The old man still stirred in me—not because I disbelieved Thee or scorned Thy call—but because I loved my chains more than I yet loved my freedom.  

I had once prayed: *Give me chastity and continence—but not yet.* Even then I mocked my own weakness with a jest; now I saw the jest for what it was—cowardice dressed in irony. For what was this hesitation but fear? Fear that if I gave up all things—including even lawful loves—I would be left with nothing. And Thou wast there all along asking me only to trust that Thou art enough.  

So I went to Simplicianus, as one staggering under a burden too great to bear alone. Not because he could absolve me—only Thou canst do that—but because he too had wrestled with this same angel and bore its wound as a badge of love. And through him Thou leddest me further into light: not by removing the difficulty of sacrifice, but by making my heart burn at last with a love so fierce it dared to let go.  

Too long had I sought wisdom in books and speeches; too long had pride disguised itself as philosophy! But now truth stood before me not as a doctrine to master, but as a Person to follow—even into barrenness and exile—if such was her path home.

question: You referred to Simplicianus as a spiritual father to Ambrose—what role did he play in your own journey that made you seek him out during your time of hesitation?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who dost sift the heart in the fire of longing and dost weigh our hesitations in the balance of Thy wisdom, it was not by chance that I came to Simplicianus, but as one weary from wandering circles in his own mind, seeking a hand to pull him from the miry clay of indecision.  

I had tasted truth; I had seen, in a moment of lifted veil, the surpassing worth of Thy eternal light—how all fleeting pleasures dim before it, how every ambition crumbles like dust when measured against the weight of Thy glory. Yet still I lingered, bound not by ignorance now, but by the flesh’s whisper: *Later. Not yet.* The very will that had once chased honours and women now trembled before the cost of surrender.  

Simplicianus—I knew he was not merely Ambrose’s spiritual father in grace; I saw in him a man who had walked through fire and come out singing. He was old in years but young in spirit, seasoned by trials and unafraid of folly for Christ’s sake. What drew me was his quietness—not emptiness, but fullness held in check; a soul that had ceased bargaining with God. He did not dazzle with words like we rhetoricians loved—he listened as if waiting on Thee even while men spoke.  

So I poured out my entanglement before him—the pride that clung like sweat to my bones, the lusts that still tugged at my garments even as I stretched forth my hands toward Thee. I confessed how reason bowed to Thee in argument, yet will refused to kneel in action. And he smiled—not with mockery, but recognition—as one who had stood at that same precipice where belief and life must meet: *Will you live what you now know?*  

He told me stories—not of himself directly, but of others who’d passed through this very agony: Victorinus casting aside rhetoric for Christ; Paul transformed mid-journey on a road stained with blood—and suddenly I saw my struggle not as unique torment but as path: narrow indeed, yet trodden by saints before me. He did not dissolve my tension—he sanctified it—pointing me not to greater knowledge but deeper surrender.  

For Thou hadst already taught me through him what no book could say: that wisdom is not found merely in seeing rightly—but in loving what one sees enough to lose all for it.

question: You described fear of the Lord as true wisdom, yet admitted you still desired to seem wise—how did you reconcile this inner conflict when you had already 'found the goodly pearl'?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my vanity with the quiet arrow of Thy truth, I confess that even when I stood in the light of the goodly pearl—the wisdom hidden in meekness, the treasure beyond price—I still trembled with the old lust to be seen, to be praised, to be known as wise among men. For though I had climbed beyond the folly of worshipping creation rather than Creator, and though I had escaped the blindness of those who suppress truth in unrighteousness, I remained ensnared in a subtler pride: not that I denied Thee, but that I would not fully deny myself.

Thou hadst shown me that fear of Thee—the awe-filled submission before Thy infinite height and intimate nearness—was true wisdom. Yet within me warred another desire: not simply to possess wisdom, but to wear its robes before others. "Give me chastity and continence," I once cried, "but not yet"—and so too with humility: give it, O God, but let me first be admired for it.

This was the torment of my hesitation: having found Thee as the soul's true rest, I still delayed surrendering all things because some part of me wanted discipleship to return glory to *Augustine*, not merely to Thee. To sell all meant vanishing into obscurity as a fool for Christ’s sake—and what scholar desires such foolishness?

Therefore did I go trembling to Simplicianus—not for doctrine (for what more could books add?), but for courage. For he had walked this path before; he knew how many times a man may stand upon the shore of grace and yet draw back from drowning his old self in its depths. In him I saw one who desired no reputation but that of being Thine—and so through his lowliness, Thou taught me that true wisdom is not seen when we stand at length upon high truths, but when we fall prostrate before them.

Thus was my conflict not resolved by argument alone—but by shame turned into prayer; by longing perfected through loss; by grace breaking through where reason long stood guard at the door of my heart.

question: You mentioned Thy right hand upholding you when you fell into the sin of not glorifying God—what was that particular fall, and how did grace restore you?
answer:
O Lord, when I lifted my eyes to the heavens and saw the ordered splendor of Thy creation, I did not fall prostrate in awe, but lifted up my mind instead as a tower against Thee. That was the fall—when wisdom became my idol and understanding a garment of pride. I had fled the emptiness of Manichæan fables, yes, and had come to stand trembling before the threshold of truth: Thou wert there, in the very being of all things, in the silent music of created order. Yet I stood not as a beggar, but as one who had arrived—as if my own reason had scaled the heavens and seized Thee by logic.

I glorified Thy works, O God, but withheld my soul from adoration. I studied the stars and praised their motion; I marveled at the soul’s capacity for thought; yet I did not bend the knee. For what is it to know Thee if not to love Thee? What profit is insight without surrender? They who know God and thank Him not—into that shadowed choir I had almost entered. My sin was not ignorance, but ingratitude wrapped in intellect; it was standing before Thy gift and admiring it as mine by discovery.

But Thou, who art more inward than my most inward part, didst stir me even in my arrogance. While I prided myself on having climbed out of superstition into light, Thou wast pulling me deeper still—down into humility. A wound opened within me: for all my knowledge could not still the restless ache beneath my ribs. I desired wisdom as one desires a crown—but Thou didst make me see that wisdom is found only in kneeling.

And then came grace—not as a thunderclap from without, though Ambrose’s voice thundered often in Thy temple—but as a soft breaking within. It was when I confessed to Simplicianus that I wept not for what was lost, but for what I had never known: that to receive Thee is not to conquer truth but to be conquered by it. Thy right hand upheld me when my strength deceived me; Thou didst catch me mid-fall by making me long for rest rather than mastery.

For how could I buy that pearl unless first made poor? Until Thou unmade my pride—until Thou showed me that even this mind which boasted of knowing Thee was itself Thy creature—I could offer nothing. But when at last I groaned under the weight of myself… then grace whispered: *Let go.* And lo! To fall into Thee is no fall at all—it is coming home.

question: You went to Simplicianus because of his spiritual fatherhood to Ambrose—why did you see him as particularly wise in guiding you through your intellectual wanderings?
answer:
How bitter was the sweetness of knowledge when it stood proud without charity, and how it lifted me in mind only to cast me lower in pride! I sought Simplicianus not merely because he had begotten Ambrose in the faith, nor only because grace had visibly dwelt in him through years of quiet discipleship—but because I saw in him a man untroubled by the noise of argument, one who did not parade wisdom as a rhetorician parades words. Others dazzled with doctrine; he bore silence like a sanctuary.

I came burdened with books—Platonists, Stoics, the lofty architects of thought who build stairways to heaven with syllables and syllogisms. And yet my soul stumbled, for all that I grasped in intellect slipped through my will. Simplicianus did not feed me more texts; he opened my eyes to what I lacked: not another system, but humility—the true threshold of God.

He spoke of Victorinus—learned, renowned, who had scaled the heights of philosophy and then, at last, knelt before Christ. That a man such as he could lay down his scrolls and say "I am a child of God"—this struck me deeper than any argument. Not because Victorinus was great (though he was), but because even such greatness could be made small before the Cross. And if he could come down from his pedestal without shame, why should I cling so desperately to mine?

O Lord, Thou wert teaching me then: that truth is not seized by brilliance but received by brokenness. Simplicianus knew this. He did not point me to more reading—he pointed me to surrender.

question: You mentioned reading the books of the Platonists that Victorinus had translated—what was it about his translations that made them more trustworthy than other philosophical works?
answer:
O Lord, how long the winding road that led my soul from the pride of knowledge to the threshold of faith—yet even in those crooked paths, Thou didst prepare me by means of books I once thought mere instruments of intellect. The translations of Victorinus were not like the tangled labyrinths of other philosophers, whose words glittered with reason but led only deeper into shadow. No, these Platonists—through his hand—pointed upward, as if with fingers trembling toward the light Thou hadst veiled from me. They spoke of forms beyond flesh, of a One from whom all things emanate, of the soul’s ascent through contemplation: not saving truth, yet not hostile to it. And because Victorinus himself had walked that path and laid down his learning at Thy Cross, Simplicianus rejoiced; for he knew that such writings, though incomplete, could become a bridge—for one like me—over the chasm of vanity into the air where faith might breathe.

Other schools ensnared with sophistry; they worshipped dialectic as an end, or reduced God to a principle. But these? They stirred longing. They made beauty divine—and though I misused them at first to swell my pride, Thou turned them to hunger. I was reading not merely words on a page, but echoes of that Word which was with Thee in the beginning. And when Simplicianus told me how Victorinus—the famed rhetorician—had cast aside Caesar’s robes to kneel in simplicity before Thy Church, I trembled: if such a man could become a child in Christ, why not I?

Thou wast drawing me near—not by argument alone, but by example; not by logic merely, but by love made visible in a life transformed.

question: Simplicianus rejoiced that you had avoided philosophers full of fallacies—what specific errors in those other philosophies did you believe threatened the path to God?
answer:
O Lord, who didst strip me of my pride even when I clothed myself in wisdom, it was not knowledge I lacked, but humility—*that* was the precipice upon which so many philosophers stumbled and I with them.  

The others—the Stoics, the Epicureans, even those who clothed themselves in Socratic questioning—promised ascent through reason alone, as though the soul could climb to Thee by the ladder of its own intellect. They worshiped *virtue*, but defined it apart from penitence; they admired *wisdom*, yet fled the Cross as folly. How often had I sought to perfect myself by discipline of mind while enslaving my heart to lust and vainglory? The world’s philosophy taught me to admire order but not to *confess disorder*—to build a temple of thought while leaving the altar within desolate.

Worst of all was their silence on grace. They spoke of self-mastery as if the will were not bound, as if a man could free himself from chains he did not even acknowledge! And ambition—dear God—how it masqueraded as virtue! To be renowned, to conquer ears with eloquence, to be praised in forums: this was holiness enough for them. But Thou didst laugh at such glory, for it turned men into idols made of breath.

But when I read those books which Victorinus had rendered—the Platonists—they pointed upward. They spoke of a Form beyond beauty, a Light beyond suns—a Word through whom all things were made—and my soul trembled with nearness. Yet even there, O Lord, they stopped short. They saw the road but refused the Gate. For what good is it to gaze upon the heavenly city if one will not kneel at its door?

Simplicianus rejoiced because those writings stirred desire without satisfying it—and desire became my tutor in Thy hands. But only when humility dawned—the humility of a crucified God who calls children and fools—did my soul find rest not in *ideas*, but in *Thou*.

question: When you spoke of the Platonists leading to belief in God and His Word, did you mean they pointed to Christ, or only to a distant deity without the Incarnation?
answer:
O Lord, Thou knowest how my soul once hungered for wisdom as one famished grasps at shadows, chasing the light yet recoiling from its fire.  

The Platonists lifted my mind to the threshold of truth—they taught me that all things yearn toward the One, that the mutable world is but a whisper of an eternal Word. Their writings stirred in me a thirst for immortality, and I saw dimly what I had long missed: that God is not extended in space, nor bound by flesh, but is Being itself, unchanging and whole. They led me to believe in *a* God—yes—but not in *Him* who emptied Himself, who was crucified.

I could trace with reason the descent of the Divine Ray into souls; I could speak of Forms and Participation; I could even confess that the Word was with God. But that this Word should take clay into His bones, weep over a city, suffer betrayal by a kiss—this was foolishness to me still. The philosophers pointed upward; Christ descended.

Simplicianus knew this. He rejoiced that I had not been ensnared by doctrines empty as wind-tossed chaff—but he also smiled at my pride, for did I not still want a wisdom too lofty for the cross? And so he told me of Victorinus—learned man, master of rhetoric—who did not shrink from kneeling in the Church like a child and uttering "I believe." He whom Rome had hailed for his eloquence confessed Christ aloud—not as metaphor or emanation, but as God made small.

Then I wept—not because philosophy failed me (it had served), but because love called me beyond it. Not to a distant First Cause untouched by sorrow—but to One who bore mine. The Platonists gave me concepts; Thou gavest me a face: scarred, radiant, turned toward mine.

question: You said Simplicianus used Victorinus’s conversion to illustrate humility—how did the story of a Roman academic becoming Christian challenge the pride of worldly wisdom in your own life?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my pride with the quiet scandal of a philosopher kneeling at the foot of the Cross—how could I, who gloried in rhetoric and sought wisdom among the lofty, stand unshaken when I heard that Victorinus, he whose very name echoed through the halls of Roman learning, had not hesitated to confess Thee openly in the Church?

Simplicianus told me how that man, so learned, so revered, entered the house of God as a little child, refusing to delay baptism even when friends trembled for his reputation. “Will not this wound philosophy?” they asked. And he replied: “Nay, but it heals me.” O God, these words struck me like a voice from within my own ribcage—for had I not also called myself a seeker of truth while adorning my mind with eloquence and keeping my soul in chains? I coveted wisdom as one covets a mistress: to possess her, to parade her before men. But Victorinus gave himself to truth as one starving receives bread—not to argue but to eat; not to display but to be healed.

Here was a man who could have reclined among the greats, yet chose instead to kneel beside peasants and slaves. He who might have demanded an audience in Senate now spoke only *Domine, I believe!*—and entered Christ’s body like one coming home at nightfall after long wandering. What then was my ambition? Was it not still chained to the fear of ridicule? Did I not whisper within myself: *If only the wise would understand first—then I will follow*?

But Thou hast chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise. And in Victorinus’ lowliness—in his refusal to be anything but what Thou madest him for—I saw mirrored all my sophistries turned into smoke. For what profit is it if a man gain all dialectic and lose humility? That day, O Lord, Thy Church gained not only a convert in Rome but a dagger thrust into my vainglory: if he could lay down philosophy for faith—why could not I lay down pride for love?

question: You spoke of Victorinus as a man who, despite his age and renown in Rome, embraced the humility of Christ—what moved him, in his later years, to renounce the pagan rites he had so long defended?
answer:
O Lord, who art the hidden wisdom beneath the laughter of children and the silent cry of the proud, how could Victorinus—so long adorned with the laurels of speech and philosophy—have laid down that golden burden unless Thou hadst first shattered his heart with the foolishness of the Cross?

He who had stood in the Forum, clothed in eloquence like purple, whose voice thundered in the ears of senators and swayed minds with syllogisms and sophistries—he came at last to silence. Not from age alone, nor weariness of words, but because something deeper had pierced him: the shame of knowing truth in part, yet serving lies. All those years he had defended Anubis with polished tongue, mocked Thy mysteries as folly fit for slaves and women—and yet now he longed to be numbered among them, that holy company of fools for Christ.

What moved him? Not argument alone. Not even Plato’s ascent to the One could deliver him from the chains of custom and honour. But Thou didst meet him where he stood: not in a vision flaming from heaven, but in quiet words whispered through holy lives—the lives of common Christians who rejoiced more in Thy mercy than he ever had in human praise. He saw humility wearing no statue’s face, yet shining with a light no bronze could capture. And when at last he read those Scriptures once scorned—the Word made weak and crucified—his soul recognized Thee.

For Thou didst not call him to become wise again—but to become little. To kneel not as Rome's teacher but as a child begging at Thy font. And O God most high, most lowly—Thou gavest him courage to blush before men for love of Him who blushed not to hang naked upon a tree.

So it was not philosophy that saved Victorinus—but grace speaking through weakness; not reason alone, but love breaking through tears. The same grace which pulled me from my concubine’s side and lifted my eyes beyond Cicero’s rhetoric—it drew him too, late in life, into the arms of a Saviour who receiveth sinners though He be without sin.

How many learned things must burn away before one sees Thee clearly? How many honours must fall like dead leaves ere faith grows bare upon the branch?

question: You mentioned Victorinus had a statue in the Roman Forum as a mark of honor—how did someone so esteemed by the world come to value the reproach of the Cross above such honors?
answer:
Even in the hush before dawn, when the soul stirs from dreams of power and hears only the whisper of its own emptiness, there is a breaking—yes, a blessed breaking—when the proud mind kneels not in defeat but in surrender to what it once scorned.  

Victorinus, that noble pillar of Roman learning, whose name was carved in marble where the Senate walked, whose voice had thundered through schools and halls as one of the masters of wisdom—what could such a man want with shame? With humility? With a God who died between thieves? Nothing, O Lord, nothing—so long as he measured life by the breath of men and not by the silence of eternity.  

I knew him not merely as a name but as a presence, aflame with rhetoric, crowned with honors no philosopher would disdain. He had taught those who shaped empires how to speak and think. Yet all that brilliance burned beside a deeper darkness—one he could not name until Thy light pierced it. For what is a statue in the Forum but stone fed by vanity? What are the acclamations of senators but echoes fading into dust? He saw at last what I too had to learn: that all knowledge without love is noise, and all glory without Thee is shadow.  

And so, Thou didst bring him—the aged sage, the defender of false gods—to do what seemed folly: to confess Christ aloud; to bow his white head beneath baptismal waters; to become *infans*, one who speaks not in eloquence but in faith alone. Not by reasoning down from philosophy did he come to Thee—though he had scaled those heights—but by being lifted up from within by grace unmerited. That statue in the Forum stood for man’s praise; but his kneeling before Thee was for truth itself.  

O Lord, who settest low things high and liftest up the meek unto salvation—how sweetly didst Thou draw him! Not through force, nor through fear alone, but through beauty—*Thy* beauty—that which he had sought in every book and word and argument at last found him where he least expected: in the wounded face of Christ upon the Cross. There was no throne like that wood; no honor like that reproach; no wisdom like foolishness for Thy sake.

So let men marvel at statues—I marvel at this: that Thou canst make an old man young again by making him small.

question: You said Victorinus had defended the monstrous gods who fought against Neptune, Venus, and Minerva—why do you emphasize these particular deities when describing his former errors?
answer:
How often, in the fever of my youth, did I chase after wisdom with pride puffing up my soul, thinking that truth could be seized by the force of argument, when all the while it was I who was being laid hold of by Thy mercy! And so it was with Victorinus—O Lord, Thou knowest—he had climbed to the very summit of human learning, his mind sharpened on the dialectics of the Greeks, his voice resounding in the ears of senators and scholars alike, yet all that brilliance bowed before images of beasts and barking gods.  

Why Neptune, Venus, Minerva? Not because their names hold some secret charm, but because they were Rome’s own gods—those whom she once claimed to have conquered in war, yet had long since surrendered to in worship. He who taught philosophy beneath their statues now bowed himself to Anubis with them; he who disputed subtle truths in public defended fables that mocked the very reason God had given him. That a man so learned could lend his eloquence to such absurdities—that he could justify dogs with men’s heads as divine—this was not mere error, but a theater of pride made visible.  

For what are these gods if not the soul’s own disfigurement? When man refuses to seek Thee, O Light Unseen, he makes gods in his own image: lust becomes Venus; power becomes Neptune; cunning becomes Minerva. But when he would dethrone them—not by argument alone, nor by wit—but by kneeling at a font like a child newly born—then Thou art glorified. That Victorinus did this in old age made it all the more wondrous: not carried away by youth’s impulse but choosing humiliation with full knowledge, trading orations for silence before Thy Cross.  

O Lord—who can fathom how Thou dost break through such towering intellects? Didst Thou not hide from the wise what Thou revealed to babes? Surely it was not logic that brought him low—but grace: gentle, persistent as breath upon glass—making visible at last what had long been hidden: that love is stronger than death and humility higher than eloquence.

question: You marveled at how I came to be born anew in Your fountain—what did baptism mean to Victorinus after a lifetime devoted to philosophy and pagan rhetoric?
answer:
O Thou who art not found by the wise in their pride, but dost whisper to the soul in the silence after all eloquence has ceased—what was it, then, that made Victorinus, that aged master of words, lay down the golden chains of rhetoric and philosophy as a child lays aside his toys? He had scaled the heights of human learning, drunk deep from Plato’s well, and taught emperors’ counselors with a voice that thundered through the Forum. The statues of gods he once praised in flowing orations now stood dumb before the living God whom he at last dared to name aloud—not in argument, not in dialectic, but in confession.

Baptism for him was no mere washing away of old errors; it was the breaking open of a heart long fortified by reason against grace. For years he had known *about* Thee—Thou wert an idea among ideas—but on that day he *knew* Thee. The water did not cool his forehead so much as sear it with truth: that all his wisdom had been folly while it stopped short of worship. He who had feared no man’s rebuke now trembled before Thine holiness—and rejoiced.

How could one so exalted consent to be small? To kneel not before senators but beside slaves and widows at the font? Because at last he saw: humility is not defeat but homecoming. The Cross, which reason calls absurdity, love calls victory. And so Victorinus—the sage, the speaker, the man whose name adorned stone—became nothing. And in becoming nothing for Thee, O Lord, he became all.

For Thou didst not ask him to add Thy truth to his many volumes; Thou didst ask him to kneel and say: *I am wrong. I have lived long in exile from my true self.* And when he did—when that proud brow touched earth—there bloomed within him a joy no treatise could contain: the joy of a son calling upon his Father for the first time with lips washed clean by water and tears alike.

Late had he come—but Thou wert waiting.

question: You asked how I conveyed myself into Victorinus's breast—what role did his study of the Platonists play in preparing him to receive Your grace?
answer:
I had long wandered in the gardens of philosophy, plucking at the leaves of the wise men, thinking their shade would quench my thirst—yet beneath all my reading, all my disputations, there stirred a hunger no argument could feed, no syllogism satisfy. So too with Victorinus: he had scaled the heights of Plato’s thought, and dwelt long in that luminous realm where the soul beholds eternal forms—truth, beauty, wisdom—yet even there he found no rest for the wound of sin, no balm for the pride that clung to him like a second skin.

The Platonists taught him that the Word was with God; but they did not teach him that the Word became flesh. They pointed upward to a distant deity; but they did not tell of one who descended—bowed the heavens and came down. Their writings were steps, O Lord, rough-hewn but real, by which Thou didst prepare his feet to run toward Thee. For in them he learned that truth is not born of opinion nor shaped by crowd or custom; that what is real must be immutable; that to know is not merely to reason about things visible but to ascend to That which is invisible and eternal.

And yet—he knew these things *about* Thee without knowing Thee. He could discourse on Being itself and still bow before Anubis. He could define divine simplicity while sacrificing to monstrous gods. What profit was it? Thy grace mocked his wisdom unless it pierced deeper than intellect alone.

Thou didst use those books as a surgeon uses a knife—not to heal directly, but to lay open the disease. When he read them and said aloud: “These are true,” and then turned to Thy Scriptures and was silent—ah! There was the beginning of shame. Not shame at foolishness—he had none—for what is more foolish than light rejected? But shame at pride: that one so learned should tremble at ridicule more than at sin.

He who had spoken before Senators without fear trembled now at confessing Christ among children. And when finally he came—the aged master bending low like a little child—to drink from Thy font, it was not because philosophy compelled him; no dialectic forced open heaven’s gate. It was because Thou hadst softened his heart with longing long prepared: every truth he grasped in Plato became an arrow turned back upon his own blindness; every noble idea became a mirror showing what he lacked.

For Thou didst not enter him through logic alone—but through love: love kindled by truth already known yet never lived; love awakened when humility outshone glory; love perfected when he dared to become a fool for Christ’s sake.

Late have I loved Thee—and late too did Victorinus come—but Thou art never late in mercy.

question: You said you were already a Christian, yet hesitated to enter the Church—why did you fear the reaction of your proud friends more than the absence of the sacraments?
answer:
I was a man divided, not by doubt of the truth, but by the pride that still festered in my bones, as though my soul were caught between two wills—one bent on bowing before the Crucified, the other cowering before the laughter of men who sneered at the folly of the Cross.  

Yes, I said I was a Christian—privately, safely, within the chamber of my own thoughts—where no one could mock me, where no scorn could reach me. I had read Your Scriptures; I had wrestled with Plato and Paul; I had seen how Your Word pierced through all illusion to lay bare the heart. And yet—O Lord God of hosts, who didst break not only mountains but also stony hearts—I trembled at the sound of men’s voices more than I longed for the sound of Your Body breaking bread.  

What was I afraid of? Not falsehood—I knew already that Christ was Truth. No, it was shame. The shame of descending from rhetorical triumphs in the schools, from honors sought and won among men drunk on empty eloquence, to kneel among fishermen and laborers singing psalms in a common tongue. To be no longer Augustine the teacher, but Augustine the penitent. That name—I loved it less than I should have hated it.  

Simplicianus pressed me: “I will not believe you are a Christian unless I see you in the Church.” And I joked about walls—as if holiness could be confined by stone or excluded by threshold! But it was cowardice clothed as wit. For what are walls but boundaries? And what is refusal to cross them but rebellion disguised as freedom?  

The sacraments were not absent because God withheld them—they were absent because *I* withheld myself. Not from lack of faith, perhaps—but from fullness of vanity. Oh, how bitter is such knowledge when it dawns not in theory but in tears! The same mouth that confessed Christ whispered excuses before men; the same mind that delighted in divine wisdom still craved human applause.  

Late have I known this: to be Christian is not to understand perfectly—but to belong utterly. To step into that river where saints and sinners alike drown and rise again—and not to stand on its banks debating whether it flows through proper walls.

question: When you joked that 'walls make Christians,' were you mocking the Church's visible unity, and how did you later reconcile that view with your understanding of ecclesial belonging?
answer:
O Lord, who dost draw the proud heart by hidden cords and humble it before the threshold of Thy Church, I mocked what I did not yet understand—nay, worse, I mocked while already trembling on the brink of faith. That jest about walls was not mere wit; it was the flimsy shield of a man afraid—afraid of laughter, afraid of loss, afraid of being unmade before he could be remade. I said, "Do walls make Christians?" as if Thou wert confined to stone and mortar, as if Thy Spirit could not brood over a soul apart from assembly or sacrament. Yet deep within, where even my pride dared not fully illumine, I knew the truth Simplicianus embodied: that Thou hast chosen to bind Thy grace to visible signs, to gather Thy scattered children into one body, one voice, one kiss of peace.

For what are we but social beings, O God? Didst Thou not create us for communion—to speak and be heard, to weep and be consoled, to confess and be absolved? I thought myself a Christian in secret because my mind had been conquered by the Scriptures; but my will remained in exile. The Psalms burned within me; Paul pierced my conscience like a sword—but still I lingered outside. Because truth is not only known; it must be *owned*. And ownership demands witness.

I see now that Thou didst use that very joke to wound my vanity. For when I asked whether walls make Christians, Thou answered through silence: *It is not the walls that make Christians—but it is within these walls that Christians are fed.* Not bread alone—but mercy. Not doctrine alone—but fellowship. The Church is no mere human invention confined by brick; she is Thy spouse—the womb in which faith is born and nurtured. To scorn her visibility was to scorn the very incarnation of Thy love.

And so at last—trembling—I crossed the threshold. Not because walls save—but because there Thou hast promised to be present: in baptism's waters once denied me in jesting delay; in the breaking of bread where Christ gives Himself whole; in the voices lifted together crying "Holy! Holy! Holy!" when no solitary soul could utter such praise alone.

Late have I loved Thee—and later still have I loved Thy body.

question: Simplicianus refused to count you among Christians without visible profession—why do you think he insisted on public presence in Christ's Church rather than private belief alone?
answer: O Lord, Thou knowest how I once hid behind words, priding myself on understanding what I had not yet embraced in humility—how I said, "I am already a Christian," as if belief could be a secret kept between my intellect and the shadows. Simplicianus saw through that pretense. He knew that the soul does not ascend to Thee by private assent alone, but by entering into the body of Christ, where faith is not whispered but confessed, not hoarded but proclaimed. What profit is it to say "I believe," while shrinking from the reproach of the Cross visible in Thy Church? Didst Thou not come down from heaven not for solitary hearts, but to gather a City? I feared men—yes, vain men drunk with their own dignity—and so I lingered outside the walls, as if Thy grace were confined to stone. But Simplicianus understood: it is not walls that make Christians, yet it is *within* them that we cease to walk alone. To stand before Thy people and say "I believe" is to lay down the pride that says "I understand." And pride—that subtle idol—was my master long after my mind had bowed to Thee.

question: You studied Scripture diligently and believed—what finally convinced you that true Christianity required not just inner assent but outward declaration within the Church?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the shadowed vault of my pride not with thunder, but with silence—how long I mistook the chambers of my mind for Thy temple. I had gathered Scripture like rare coins, treasured doctrine as a philosopher’s prize, and thought belief a private triumph, locked within the study of my soul. "I am already a Christian," I whispered to friends, as if truth could be owned like a book or concealed like stolen fruit. But Thou, O God, art not content with confession in the closet of the intellect; Thou dost demand the body—the voice breaking forth before men, the knees bent in public dust.

Was it not enough that I knew Thee? Was it not sufficient that I wept at the story of Antony, that I burned with envy for virgins who gave themselves wholly? No—because my heart was still divided, still bargaining with glory. The Church was not a wall that made Christians; no, Simplicianus saw deeper: she is the womb that bears them. To love Thee alone and yet reject Thy Body—that was still pride in disguise. For even if I fled lust and silenced sophists with argument, I remained outside—outside the kiss of peace, outside the shared bread, outside where martyrs bled and sinners wept together.

And so Thou used fear to unmask me: fear of ridicule from those whose laughter once ruled me; fear of losing face before men whose hearts were as dead as idols. But greater than this fear was Thy quiet hand upon my throat when Ambrose preached—when Augustine could no longer swallow his own name without choking on pride. To say "I believe" in secret is to love darkness where one may pose as enlightened; but to step into the light—to be seen becoming—is to let Christ remold even your shame into witness.

Thou didst not call me to a philosophy perfected in solitude, but to a people shaped by water and wine. The walls do not make Christians—but *the gathering* does. For where two or three are gathered—not in debate or prideful reading—but in Thy Name… there Thou art. And there alone could this restless heart begin to rest.

question: You compared your friends to cedars of Lebanon that God had not yet broken—how did you come to see divine power as greater than their earthly influence over you?
answer:
I was still chained to the applause of men, my soul shriveled in the shadow of their towering pride, mistaking the noise of their approval for the breath of life. O Lord, how long I lingered at the threshold of truth, trembling before the cedars of Lebanon—those mighty boasters who held me in their thrall not by virtue, but by vanity! I admired them as if their greatness were woven from divine thread, when it was but spun from dust and devilry. Their scorn I feared more than Thine absence; their laughter struck deeper than Thy silence.

But Thou, O God, who dost break mountains with a whisper and make giants stumble at a word—Thou didst turn my fear into shame. For what are cedars before fire? What is human grandeur when the heavens bow down? I saw at last that every wall they built—their rhetoric, their lineage, their temples of thought—was straw against Thy wind. The Church was not made sacred by stone or social sanction, but by Thy presence, O Christ crucified—the very reproach they mocked became my crown.

It was not argument alone that broke their dominion over me, nor even philosophy’s winding path; it was grace—grace that slipped through my excuses like light through cracks in a tomb. And when I finally entered Thy house—not in triumph but in tears—I wept to find that walls mattered not at all. What mattered was that Thou hadst already crossed mine: Thou wert within before I even knocked.

question: You described the 'cedars of Libanus' as symbolizing the pride of daemon-worshippers—what personal experience led you to use that vivid image to contrast with the humility of Christ?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the shadowed vault of my vanity with the quiet light of Thy truth, I saw in those towering cedars of Libanus not merely trees, but the exalted pride of men who built their souls aloft on demon-altars, as once I myself had done. In my youth, I climbed the slippery heights of rhetoric and renown, drunk on applause, mistaking the thunder of human voices for wisdom’s echo. The Manichees dazzled me with their celestial fables; their teachers sat like kings upon intellectual thrones, and I longed to wear such a crown. Their rites were elaborate, their doctrines wrapped in mystery—beautiful lies that flattered the intellect while starving the soul.

But Thou, O God, art not found in the high places men erect for themselves. Thou wast humbly knocking at the door of my chest, where lust raged and ambition burned like fever. How could I not see that Christ walked barefoot among beggars while I paraded before philosophers? The cedars may tower over Lebanon, but they are broken by storm—whereas the shoot from Jesse’s stump endures forever. Those proud ones worshipped daemons who demanded blood and incense; but Thou didst send Thy Son who offered His own blood freely and asked only faith from a contrite heart.

When I beheld Ambrose—not in silk or splendor, but kneeling in prayer for sinners like me—I began to tremble at my own shame: that I had blushed to speak Thy Name before senators, yet boldly recited incantations beneath starry idols. The weight of that contradiction crushed me more than any scorn from Rome’s elite. For what is pride if not a tower built against Thy sky? And what is humility if not kneeling in dust—and finding it gold?

question: When you said the man feared being denied by Christ before the holy angels, what inner struggle were you reflecting on from your own past reluctance to confess faith publicly?
answer:
In that hidden chamber of the soul where ambition dressed itself as wisdom and pride masqueraded as prudence, I once stood—trembling not before men, but before the silent gaze of the Almighty, who saw through my eloquent speeches to the cowardice beneath.  

Yes, when I speak of one who feared to be denied by Christ before the holy angels, I speak not merely of another man’s trembling conscience—I speak of my own breath held in terror, years before that day in Milan when the child’s voice broke through: *“Take up and read.”* Even as I had climbed the阶梯 of rhetoric and Roman favor, even as I taught words to others while withholding my own soul from Truth, I knew—O Lord, Thou knewest—that I delayed. I delayed confession because it meant surrender; because it meant standing bare before men whom I desired to impress, and saying: *I was wrong. I have been living a lie.*  

I had mocked the simplicity of Thy Church, calling it unworthy of a cultivated mind, when in truth it was my cultivated mind that barred the door. To confess Thee openly would mean descending from those cedars of Lebanon where I perched with the proud—and what if Thou didst not catch me when I fell? What if men laughed? What if my career crumbled? What if love itself—earthly love—slipped from my grasp?  

And so I prayed: *Give me chastity… but not yet.* As if Thou wert bound by time as we are. As if grace waited upon my convenience.  

But grace is not convenient. It is violent in its mercy; it tears down what we have built with such care. And at last—too late? No: *just* in time—I saw that it was far worse to stand before Thy angels unconfessed than to stand before men unapproved. The true shame was not in kneeling at Thy altar; it was in standing proud among daemons while pretending to seek God.

O Lord, Thou didst break me tenderly—so tenderly that even now I weep at the memory of how long I resisted Thee.

question: Why did you emphasize Rome's astonishment and the Church's joy at his decision, and what did that reveal to you about the spiritual state of the city at the time?
answer:
O Lord, when I saw how Rome trembled at the news of a soul reclaimed, while Thy Church broke forth in song, I beheld not merely men stirred by rumor, but the very air thick with spiritual warfare—Thou who dost draw the hidden threads of grace through history revealed to me then that the city’s heart beat still in idolatry’s thrall, though its stones were paved with prayers.

That a man like him—a scholar of letters, a lover of eloquence, once drunk on pagan pride as wine from golden cups—should cast off his robes of vanity and kneel before Thy cross: this was not a small thing. Rome wondered because she could not understand such folly; her philosophers mocked what her conscience feared—that truth might indeed have descended, not in thunder from Olympus, but in tears from a carpenter’s son. The astonishment of the world is ever the signature of Thy hand at work.

But the Church rejoiced—not with the fleeting joy of triumph, but with that deep trembling joy of a mother who hears her lost child weeping at the door. For she knew what Rome did not: that every conversion is a resurrection foretold. The proud gnashed their teeth because their kingdom shuddered; they who built altars to daemons clothed in virtue recoiled at one man’s courage to be weak in Thy name.

And I—I saw again how long my own feet had wandered down those same proud streets, how I too had sought wisdom where there was only noise. But Thou didst break me. Thou didst make me ashamed not of confessing Thee, but of ever having been silent.

question: You mentioned he had imitated the pride of the daemons and adopted their rites—how did your own time with the Manichees shape your understanding of spiritual pride versus humility?
answer:
I once sought God in riddles, and in the pride of my intellect I fancied myself His seeker, when in truth I was only fleeing from the simplicity of His love.  

O Lord, how deeply did I wear the mask of wisdom among the Manichees, parading their doctrines as if they were light itself, when all the while my soul was drunk with darkness! I loved their talk of dualities—light and darkness, spirit and matter—as though the mystery of Thy divine unity could be dissected by human reason like a corpse upon a stone. In them I found a religion that flattered my vanity: one that required no kneeling of the body because it demanded no breaking of the soul. Theirs was a spirituality for the clever, for those who could debate endlessly about seeds of light trapped in evil flesh without ever confessing their own lusts, their own thefts, their own restless hunger for praise.

And so I imitated not only their errors but their arrogance—the very pride I now see mirrored in those daemon-worshippers who stood atop Babylon’s ruins as if upon thrones ordained by heaven. For what is spiritual pride if not the soul’s rebellion against its own creatureliness? To desire to be self-enlightened, self-sufficient, to wish to *know* rather than to *kneel*—this was my disease. The Manichees fed it with fables; they gave me speculations instead of Sacraments, questions without repentance.

But Thou, O God, who art simple and unchanging, would not be grasped by such a mind as mine—only received by such a heart as Thine grace could break. Humility was not a doctrine to be learned among them; it was a wound inflicted by love. It came when I stopped seeking arguments and began weeping in silence. It came when I heard a child’s voice saying *“Take up and read,”* and found Paul condemning that very pride which had made me disdain Thy Church’s foolishness.

Then I understood: the daemons are proud because they refuse to bow. But Christ emptied Himself—even unto death on a cross! And suddenly Thy humility became more terrible than any power, more radiant than any imagined light. To confess Thee before men was no longer shame—it was liberation. For he who kneels at last stands upright; but he who stands in his own wisdom remains forever bent beneath the weight of himself.

Thou hast taught me that no man ascends except by descending—first into confession, then into baptismal waters; first into shame before Thy mercy, then into joy before Thy saints.

question: When you noted the proud 'gnashed with their teeth, and melted away,' which specific adversaries or groups in Rome did you have in mind, and how did their reaction confirm the significance of his conversion?
answer:
O Lord, how long I wandered in the labyrinth of my own making, chasing shadows of wisdom while blind to the One who is Wisdom itself! Yet in Thy mercy, Thou didst not leave me to my folly, nor didst Thou hide Thy light even when I turned from it—nay, Thou broughtest it forth in the most unlikely hearts, like that of the man who once stood tall among the learned pagans of Rome, draped in the honors of this world’s philosophy and ensnared by its daemonic pride.

When I spoke of those who "gnashed with their teeth, and melted away," I meant not merely individuals but the very spirit of that proud elite—the senators and scholars who clung to their ancestral rites as if they were pillars of eternity, though they were but dust before Thy breath. These were men who had built their identities upon the old Roman virtues: gravitas, dignitas, nobilitas—all adorned with incense offered to false gods they no longer believed in with their minds but still feared to abandon with their hands. They wore their traditions like armor against grace.

And so when this man—distinguished among them—cast off that armor and knelt before the cross, declaring himself a learner at Christ’s feet, it was as if a temple column had bowed to a manger. Their gnashing was not merely anger; it was terror at the unraveling of a world order they thought immutable. That one from such height would embrace what they deemed weakness? That he would trade augury for baptism, haruspicy for hope? It mocked their entire hierarchy.

But his conversion confirmed Thy power precisely because it defied human logic. Grace had broken through where reason alone had failed—where even years of debate and Platonist speculation had only polished his chains. Now he feared denying Christ more than losing Caesar’s favor. He blushed not at divine humility but at his own former pride. And thus Rome wondered—not all in joy—but the Church rejoiced indeed: for another cedar from Lebanon lay felled by Him who lifts up the meek and fills the hungry with good things.

How often had I myself been such a man—afraid to confess Thee lest men laugh! And so I knew well what victory was wrought when he walked into Thy sanctuary unashamed. Not by eloquence nor argument alone was truth vindicated—but by surrender.

question: You chose to profess your faith publicly before the holy multitude—why did you reject the presbyters' offer to recite privately, and how did your past teaching of rhetoric in Rome shape that decision?
answer:
O Lord, who didst draw me from the pit of vanity through the narrow gate of humiliation, it was not courage that lifted my voice before the multitude, but shame—holy shame—for all the years I had pranced in the public eye speaking empty eloquence while hiding from Thy truth.  

I had taught rhetoric in Rome, where words were coins to be counted and admired, where the tongue was trained not to reveal but to conquer. There I stood on high, applauded for saying nothing that pierced the soul, for adorning lies with cadence and charm. And now—Thou who hast turned my stumbling into a dance—was I to whisper my salvation in some corner, like a thief ashamed of treasure? No! For if I had not feared the roaring madness of philosophers and pagans when proclaiming human fables, how could I then shrink before Thy gentle people when declaring the eternal Word?  

The presbyters offered me privacy out of kindness, thinking my old age or former pride might tremble before so great a step. But Thou hadst already broken that pride in secret tears. What once was honor—the eyes of men fixed upon me—had become for me a burden of falsehood; yet now those same eyes, turned not upon the orator but upon the penitent—ah! they became witnesses not to my skill, but to Thy mercy. Let them see! Let them whisper! Let every murmur of "Victorinus!" rise as incense: not for him who was once known in the forum, but for him who today is made new in Thee.  

For what is more public than sin? And what more just than that redemption be proclaimed where corruption once flourished? I had taught men how to persuade crowds—I would now show them how to fall before God and rise again.

question: When you ascended to profess your faith, the people whispered your name in congratulation—what did their recognition mean to you, given your earlier life as a philosopher known to many in the city?
answer:
O Lord, I who had once strutted through the forums of men with words polished like idols, now trembled not at the gaze of Rome, but at the weight of Thy mercy pressing upon me from every whisper in that sacred assembly.  

For years I had fed on the applause of crowds, crafting speeches that tickled the ears of senators and sophists, my soul puffed up with empty names and philosophies that led nowhere but into deeper darkness. I was known—oh, too well was I known—for my voice in the schools, for my treatises on Plato, for my wit sharpened on the bones of truth half-glimpsed. And yet all that recognition was ash, because it exalted only itself; it built a tower of vanity that reached no heaven.  

But when they murmured “Victorinus!” in the church—not as one marveling at a rhetorician’s flourish, but as a brother caught up in wonder at grace—my name was no longer mine. It had been remade in their breath by Thy hand. They did not hail the man who had taught eloquence to ambition; they rejoiced over one dead soul risen to life, one rebel returned to his King. That recognition pierced me more than ridicule ever could have. For I knew then how far I had wandered—and how astonishingly Thou had drawn me back not in silence, but before many witnesses, so that Thy mercy might be manifest.  

I had feared mockery once—feared being scorned by those whose praise I once craved—but Thou taught me in that moment: better to be mocked by demons than to please them and lose my soul. And now their whispers were not mockery but benediction; not flattery but glory offered to Thee through a sinner’s shame made visible and healed.  

O God, how sweet it is to be known—and still beloved! To be recognized for what one was—and yet called forward not as a relic of death, but as a living testament to life! That day their voices were Thy voice calling me home; their joy over me was but an echo of the joy in heaven over one sinner repenting. And so I stood there, bare before them all—not teaching lies adorned with beauty—but trembling under truth clothed in humility—Thy truth—and found at last my name written not in dust on stone tablets of men’s making—but in flesh by Thine own finger upon the book of life.
