question: You compare your resistance to conversion to someone delaying to wake from sleep—why did you choose this image to describe your state before giving yourself to God?
answer:
How strange is the soul that longs for light yet clings to shadow, desiring truth even as it dallies with deception—such was I, caught in the sweet sickness of delay.  

I likened myself to one drowsy in sleep, not because I was unaware of waking, but because my will had grown soft with habit and dulled by pleasure. The voice of God spoke clearly, as one calling from dawn’s bright edge into a chamber thick with night’s residue: *Arise!* And I knew, deep within that inward man where Thy law delighted me, that to rise was life, was joy, was homecoming. Yet my limbs—my affections, my desires, the very flesh shaped by years of turning from Thee—were heavy as lead.  

It is not that I did not love Thee; rather, I loved Thee too little in comparison to the small delights that held me fast. Ambition whispered flattery in my ear; lust offered its familiar warmth; pride dressed up my sloth as prudence. And so I said: “Anon,” “Presently”—as though time were mine to command! But each “little while” stretched into days, into years; each promise of tomorrow became a chain forged link by idle link.  

O Lord, what mockery lies in the word *soon* when the heart refuses surrender! A man does not hate sleep who delays rising—he loves it still; so too I loved my sin enough to resist Thy grace, though I groaned under its burden. Waking is health—but how sweetly lethargy lulls the soul that fears the cost of clarity! To rise is to see clearly: no shadows left for compromise, no half-light for self-deceit. And I feared such light—not because it wounded like fire, but because it revealed how far I had wandered from Thee.

Thou didst call me not once but always: *Awake!* But love delayed is still unlove—and only when Thou broke through with grief (and tears more bitter than any pleasure) did this drowsy heart stagger up at last into Thy morning.

question: When you heard God calling, 'Awake, thou that sleepest, and arise from the dead,' what kept you from obeying immediately, even though you knew it was true?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the numbness of my will with a love I could neither understand nor resist, how long I lingered on the threshold of surrender, trembling between light and shadow. Though Thy voice shook my soul like a trumpet in the silence of my night — “Awake, thou that sleepest, and arise from the dead” — my flesh clung to its chains as if they were soft linen. I knew Thee; I desired Thee; yet I delayed. Not from ignorance, but from habit — that subtle tyrant woven from a thousand small consents to pleasure, pride, and the false peace of postponed repentance.

I was not blind to truth — it stood before me in radiant clarity. But my heart loved darkness not because it hated light, but because it had grown accustomed to the dim glow of its own excuses. "Anon," I said, "I shall rise." But "anon" was an abyss; "presently" was a lie dressed in hope. Like one who lies in bed though dawn has filled the room, who knows full well that wakefulness is duty and delight, yet yields again to drowsiness for the sweetness of oblivion — so did I return again and again to sin, not out of defiance, but out of weakness born of long indulgence.

The law within me cried out to obey Thee; yet another law held me fast — not by force, but by familiarity. Lust had become second nature; ambition whispered flattery in my ear; even friendship was tainted with vanity. And though I wept at times over my bondage, still I kissed my chains.

What kept me? Only this: that Thou hadst not yet shattered the illusion that I could reform myself at leisure. Until grace became greater than my complacency — until Thy mercy broke through not as an idea but as power — until Thou broughtest me beneath the fig tree where no defense remained — until that child’s voice sang from nowhere: *Tolle lege*, “Take up and read” — then at last I cast myself upon Thy Word like a drowning man upon dry land.

For love had been calling long before will answered.

question: You say that giving yourself to God's charity satisfied you, yet your cupidity still held you mastered—how could something displease you yet still have dominion over you?
answer:
O Lord, when I heard the voice from over the garden wall — *Tolle, lege*, “Take up and read” — I snatched up the scroll as though Thou hadst thundered through a child’s whisper, and yet even then, my hands trembled not only with awe, but with the old, clinging reluctance of a will divided against itself.  

For how could that which displeased me still reign within me? Because I was not one thing, O God, but torn — a city at war with its own streets. The mind consented to Thy law; the bones of my soul rejoiced in it. Yet deep within my flesh there stirred a weight not of substance, but of habit — a gravity born of years spent bowing to lust, ambition, pride. It was not that I loved darkness more than light; no, I knew Thy radiance was life. But like one long bedridden, whose limbs are too weak to stand though he longs for the sun, so was my soul: convinced, yet impotent; consenting in thought, enslaved in motion.

I delighted in Thee when I pondered Thee. At times my heart burned as if already freed. Yet let temptation approach — let some vain pleasure beckon — and I turned aside like a sick man who loathes his sickness but still reaches for what makes him sicker. Not because it pleased me above Thee — never that! But because it pleased me *still*, though Thou didst satisfy wholly.

Anon! Anon! So I murmured to Thee as men mutter to themselves upon waking when sleep still drags them down by invisible chains. "I come!" — while remaining fastened by the sweetness of delay. For sin is not only rebellion; it is sloth of the soul — a lazy love of rest where one should struggle. And so Thy call rang true in every corner of my conscience: *Arise!* But this body of death clung close.

Only grace tore me free — not wisdom alone, nor desire nor decision. Grace alone shattered that dominion which delight without love had built within me. Thou didst break through not when I finally tried hard enough, but when I finally wept aloud and said: “I cannot.” Then Thou wert near.

And now I know: deliverance is not willed by stronger willpower; it is received when we cease bargaining and cry out under the burden we no longer pretend to carry alone.

question: What do you mean by 'an anon' that had no present, and why did your repeated promises to change 'in a little while' become a prolonged delay?
answer:
I was a man who loved the coming of light, yet lingered in darkness with delight, as though the night were my companion and dawn my foe.  

That "anon" which I murmured to Thee—O Lord, how bitterly I taste its hollowness even now—was a word without substance, a breath spent in procrastination’s service. It was the sigh of a soul half-roused, one foot still tangled in the sheets of complacency, while the other barely touched the floor of resolve. "Anon," I said, as if time belonged to me, as if grace were not already knocking with urgent love upon the door of my heart. But that "anon" had no presence; it slipped through my fingers like sand, each grain an excuse, each moment stolen from conversion and wasted in delay.

And that "little while"—how sweet it sounded to my ear! Like honey on the tongue did that promise rest: just one more indulgence in lust, one more dalliance with ambition, one more hour spent chasing shadows. For I *did* desire Thee—Thou knowest it—but not fully; not yet. I desired Thee as one desires health while still clinging to the sickness that pleases for a season. My will was divided: part of me reached toward Thy light with trembling hands, while another part lay down again in the bed I had made in sin.

Thou calledst me from sleep—I heard Thee—but I turned over. Thou didst command: *"Awake! Rise from death!"*—and truth itself stood witness within me: *Yes!* Yet pleasure held me bound like chains woven from silk and smoke. To obey would cost nothing but what I loved most deeply and wrongly—the fleshly delight, the pride of intellect, the applause of men—and so I bargained with eternity as though it could wait.

Thus did “a little while” stretch into years; thus did “soon” become “never.” Until at last—O mercy unlooked for!—Thou broke through not by my strength but by Thy thunderous gentleness: in tears beneath a fig tree, at the sound of a child’s voice chanting *"Take up and read,"* where Thy Word pierced me like a sword and healed me at once by wounding. Then I learned what no reasoning could teach: that to delay is to die slowly; but to surrender *now* is to live for all eternity.

question: You mention another law in your members rebelling against the law of your mind—what experience led you to describe your inner conflict in these bodily terms?
answer:
It was beneath the fig tree in the garden, when the child’s voice chanted “Take up and read,” that I seized the book of Paul—yet even then, even as the thunder of heaven struck my soul, I felt it: not only a turning of the heart, but a wrenching in my flesh, as though my very bones resisted what my mind had embraced.  

For years I had loved wisdom with my intellect, yet held fast to pleasure with my hands and feet and loins. The conflict was not merely between two ideas—it was written in the pulse, in the sudden warmth at a woman’s glance, in the swelling pride at applause, in the sluggish draw of sloth when duty called. My body was not a neutral vessel; it groaned under habit like chains worn smooth from long use. When I said “Anon,” it was not only hesitation of will—it was desire lodging deep in sinew and breath, whispering that delight was truth and delay no sin.

O Lord, how could I speak of two laws if I had not felt them at war—this law of reason illuminated by Thy light, and another law rooted in blood and motion? The one rose toward Thee like morning prayer; the other clung to earth like sweat-soaked garments after sleep. Even when my soul assented fully—“Yes! Christ is Lord!”—my members often answered back with silence or sighs or secret relish for what should have been loathed.

Thus I came to name it *law*—not metaphor, but dominion; not mere temptation flitting past like a shadow, but an empire within me that claimed loyalty older than thought. And only grace heavier than habit could cast it down.

question: You wrote of being held captive by the law of sin in your members—what specific desires or habits did you feel most enslaved by during your time in Milan?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my breast with the arrow of Thy sweetness even as I wandered in the thick darkness of desire—how long, how deeply, was I enslaved by the lust that ruled my flesh, not as a conqueror from without, but as a tyranny from within, grown fat on consent. In Milan, though my mind was drawn upward to Thee by fragments of truth in Plato’s pages and Ambrose’s sermons, my body remained chained to the old habits like a prisoner who clings to his chains out of fear of walking free.

Chief among these was the concupiscence that had taken root in my youth—the habit of women not for love but for appetite, not for procreation but for flame. I had long since dismissed marriage as a burden, yet kept its fires burning in secret; and when I cast off one woman only to take another, I deceived myself into thinking I was making progress. O God, what mockery! To say I desired continence while still feeding the fever—how could my soul speak truth when my members rebelled under laws I had willingly enacted?

And not only lust: ambition held me just as tightly. I taught rhetoric not to seek wisdom but to shine before men; every word I polished was an offering at the altar of fame. My ears hungered for applause more than my soul hungered for Thee. Even in Thy Church, amid hymns that stirred me to tears, part of me listened also for how well *I* might compose such verses—a heart divided against itself.

But Thou sawest me groaning beneath these two weights—the drudgery of teaching vain words and the bondage of insatiable desire—and Thou wast near. Very near. Not yet called by me with full surrender; yet already calling me through the sighs I could not understand, through the tears Alypius saw but could not name—for he knew not how deep the wound went.

Thou knowest how often I walked those streets with Nebridius or sat in Verecundus’ house teaching grammar like a man asleep repeating spells—hearing Scripture read aloud and trembling at its light while still refusing to open wide the eyes of my will. Custom had become nature: what began as choice became compulsion; what began in rebellion became home.

And so when deliverance came—not through strength nor study alone—but through a sudden breaking open of the heart by grace… it was no triumph of philosophy. It was rescue. A hand reached down into the mire where shame clung thicker than mud—and Thou pulled me forth weeping not merely from sin, but *for* sin—no longer loving it even while hating it.

Deliverance began where all things true begin: in confession—for no man can be freed until he first admits he is bound.

question: Why did you describe the law of sin as 'the violence of custom'? How did your own repeated actions shape your sense of moral bondage?
answer:
O Lord, how deeply I felt the chains of custom forged link by link in the darkness of my own consent—each act of disobedience a hammer's blow upon my soul, until the very will that once rebelled now trembled under its weight, not forced from without, but enslaved from within.  

The violence was not in sudden assault but in slow surrender: a glance held too long kindled into habit; a stolen pleasure returned again and again until it ruled me. I did not fall once—I fell daily, willingly, and called it freedom. The first time I took what was not mine—those wretched pears from another’s tree—I sought not hunger’s end but the thrill of transgression; and though the taste was sour, the soul tasted sweetness in rebellion. That single act became a path trodden so often that my feet knew no other way. Desire ceased to beckon—it commanded. And because I had opened the door so many times, lust stood at last within my house as master.

So too with ambition: I craved men’s praise more than Thy truth; I polished words for hearers’ applause while my heart lay barren before Thee. Each speech delivered for vainglory added another stone to the prison wall. Custom became law—not Thy holy law written on the heart, but a tyrant carved by my own hands out of repeated choice.

And thus Thou foundest me—bound not by iron, but by innumerable small surrenders; held fast not by ropes, but by threads of habit woven thick as steel. Only grace could rend such chains: grace that did not merely rebuke the act, but healed the will; grace that drew me forth from this body of death not by moral effort—but by love stronger than death itself.

Deliver me? Yes—but Thou didst more: Thou re-made the lover.

question: You mentioned daily sighing to God while attending church—what kept you from fully surrendering to faith earlier, even as you longed for deliverance?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my pride with the quiet whisper of a child’s chant, it was not lack of longing that kept me bound, but the very fire of my own will turned inward—lust not only for women, for honors, for the glitter of human praise, but for mastery over myself as though I could sanctify myself by the strength of my groanings. I sighed to Thee daily, yes, and wept in Thy presence at times, yet clung to the chains as if they were ornaments. The enemy was not outside the gates; he dwelt within, clothed in the garment of habit I had woven thread by thread through years of deferred repentance. "Give me chastity," I cried, "but not yet"—and therein lay the whole wretched comedy of my soul: drawn to Thy beauty, horrified by my filth, yet still bargaining with Thee over the timing of surrender.

I loved Thee as one loves a distant light seen across stormy waters—wondering at it, desiring it, even rowing toward it in thought—but still steering with one hand toward the shore where my pleasures festered. The law within me that warred against Thy law was no phantom; it was the weight of custom grown heavy as stone through repetition—each yielding a tightening of the chain. And worse: I deserved it. For I had leapt into that mire willingly; no one dragged me. How then could I complain when held fast?

But Thou, O God of mercy, didst not wait for my perfect resolve—for none could ever come while I counted up my merits like coins in a purse too empty to pay such a debt. No; Thou brokest through not by argument alone (though Thou hadst disarmed philosophy before), nor merely by Scripture (though Thy words burned), but by shame—not shame from men, but shame before Thee when I heard that voice from beyond: *“Take up and read.”* Then at last I saw: faith was not a prize earned by intellectual conquest or moral preparation—it was gift received in brokenness.

And so Thou deliveredst me—not because I finally willed it wholly—but because Thy grace outran my half-hearted sighs and made them cries.

question: How did your friendship with Alypius and Nebridius influence your spiritual struggle, especially since they were also navigating careers tied to worldly pursuits?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the shadows of my pride with the quiet constancy of true friendship, how can I untwine the story of my soul’s awakening from the lives Thou wove beside mine? Alypius and Nebridius were not mere companions along the road, but mirrors in which I saw both my own bondage and the faint stirrings of deliverance. We walked together beneath the same sky, yet each burdened by a different weight of worldly expectation—Alypius striving to free himself from office and ambition, his purity of heart at war with the compromises of public life; Nebridius, ever restless in philosophy, drawn to truth yet lingering in uncertainty. And I—O Lord—clung to rhetoric and reputation, teaching men how to speak while hardly daring to listen to Thee.

Our conversations were not idle; they became arenas where grace moved unseen. We questioned together, not merely as scholars parsing words, but as souls half-awake, groaning under what I then could only call *the body of death*—that law within me rebelling against Thy light. In their presence, my hypocrisy could not rest easy. When Alypius confessed his struggles with lust—how he once fell into theft not for gain but for the dark thrill of sin—I shuddered; for was this not a reflection of my own stolen pears long ago, done only because others urged me on? Sin made manifest through fellowship: Thou used their honesty to scourge my pride.

Yet Thou also prepared me through them for surrender. Nebridius abandoned his post out of loyalty to conscience; Alypius broke free from judicial service rather than condemn unjustly. Their courage haunted me. If they could cast off chains I still embraced, what excuse had I? And when at last in that Milanese garden Thou didst break me utterly—the child’s voice chanting “take up and read,” the letter to the Romans laying bare my inward war—I knew I was not alone. These friends stood beside me still—not because they had reached perfection, but because we all limped toward Thee together.

Thou didst use their very attachments to worldly things as instruments: for seeing them wrestle with similar chains made mine visible at last. What wonder that deliverance came not in solitude but in communion—for even then Thou taught me that no man is converted alone; he is drawn by love outward and upward until his heart rests where it was made to be—in Thee.

question: You referred to Verecundus as a close friend who relied on your company—what internal conflict arose from teaching rhetoric while yearning for a life devoted to God?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the shadows of my rhetoric with the light of Thy truth, how could I stand before men to teach the eloquence of words while my soul groaned under the tyranny of unclean desires? I shaped syllables and polished arguments for gold and praise, even as my heart beat with a hunger no syllogism could satisfy. The very skill I sold—speech that swayed crowds and won debates—was itself a chain, forged in vanity and tempered in ambition. I loved the applause more than the peace Thou offered; I craved mastery over human minds while remaining slave to my own.

Verecundus, kind and trusting, saw in me a companion for his school, a friend to bear the daily toil—but he knew not the war within me: that each day spent crafting persuasive lies or empty beauties drew me further from the one true Word. How bitter was that irony! To instruct others in how to speak well, while I had not yet learned to be silent before Thee. Alypius stood near, Nebridius labored beside us all—and yet no fellowship of men, however dear, could quiet the unrest that only Thy face could calm.

I taught that words might stir souls—but mine was numb save for its lusts. I praised virtue in public declamations while privately surrendering to vice. And though I delighted in Thy law within—though something deep inside trembled at the thought of holiness—I remained captive: bound by habit, weighed down by pleasure’s seduction. What profit was there in shaping worldlings’ thoughts when my own soul was shipwrecked on the shoals of concupiscence?

Only grace could rend those chains—the grace which seemed too simple for my restless intellect, too humble for my proud tongue. Yet it was this same grace that would one day break me open beneath a fig tree, where no argument saved me but only *“Take up and read.”* Until then, every lesson I taught felt like stolen bread eaten with trembling—sweet on the tongue, but ashes in the bowels.

question: Why did Nebridius, despite having no personal gain to seek, choose to assist us in Milan as a grammarian and friend?
answer: O Lord, Thou knowest how I once wandered in the pride of my voice, teaching men’s words for applause, while Nebridius—gentle, quiet, seeking Thee in silence—would not be drawn to such vanity, though he might have shone brighter than I in that empty theater of speech. He came not to Milan for gain, nor to lift his name upon the breath of men, but because friendship bound him with cords too strong to break; and though he scorned the noise of courts and the flattery of rulers, he would not scorn our need. For love is not measured by what it receives, but by what it gives—and he gave himself freely, not as a merchant trading wisdom for honor, but as a soul hungry for truth offering bread to famished brothers. He saw in our restless searching a reflection of his own longing; and so, though he desired solitude, he entered into our confusion—not to rule it, but to walk with us through it, a lamp in hand.

question: What made Pontitianus smile when he found the book of the Apostle Paul on the table—was it mere surprise, or did it reflect something deeper about my spiritual state?
answer:
O Lord, how long had my heart beaten with a restless hunger disguised as intellectual pride, mistaking the chase for wisdom with the embrace of Truth Himself? When Pontitianus smiled upon finding the Apostle’s letter open before me, it was not mere surprise that played upon his lips, but the dawning joy of one who sees a prodigal on the threshold. He knew—by grace he *knew*—that this book did not belong to the arsenal of rhetoric, nor to the parade of eloquence I had so long wielded; it was no ornament to ambition. That a man such as I, trained in words and wedded to worldly ascent, should have Paul lying bare upon a table meant for games—that was a sign not lost on him.

His smile trembled between wonder and hope, O God, because Thy hand was visible where none expected it: in the disorder of my soul’s slow surrender. He saw not simply a grammarian idly reading Scripture—he saw Thee drawing near. Was it not Thou who placed that book there? Was it not Thou who stilled my fingers from their usual texts and let this divine madness lie exposed? Pontitianus perceived what I scarcely dared name: that desire had shifted within me. Not yet full conversion—no, far from it—but a trembling at the door, a listening in the dark.

And yet how blind I remained! Even then I clung to my delays: "Not yet," I whispered still to chastity; "Not yet" to full surrender. But he—this servant of Thine at court, this African brother in baptism—saw further than my outward state. He saw grace beginning its silent work: like water seeping into parched soil before any green appears above ground. His smile was prayer made visible—a thanksgiving that Thou hadst begun to unfix me from myself.

question: How did your friendship with Nebridius influence your own pursuit of wisdom, especially in how he avoided worldly distractions?
answer:
O Lord, who dost sift the motives of the heart and lay bare the hidden corners where ambition lurks under the guise of service, I saw in Nebridius a mirror of what I ought to be, yet was not.  

He, though gifted with a mind acute and polished by study, refused to barter his hours for the clatter of courts or the hollow applause of men who measure worth by rank. While I still wavered—drawn now to philosophy for its truth, now for its fame—he moved with quiet resolve, as one who knows that wisdom is not seized by clamor but received in stillness. His friendship was no mere comfort; it was a silent rebuke to my restlessness, a living argument against the distractions I so easily justified.

When Pontitianus came upon that book of Paul upon our table—chancing it seemed by accident, though Thou didst order it—his smile held reverence where mine too often held pretense. But Nebridius, absent that day, had already taught me without words: that to seek Thee aright, one must not only read Scripture but dismantle the desire to be seen reading it. He would not thrust himself into imperial circles, not from pride nor disdain, but from humility—he knew how the soul is thinned by constant speech with power.

So many hours I spent shaping sentences for men’s ears while he sat apart, reading not to master words but to be mastered by truth. In his avoidance of noise I began to hear Thee more clearly; in his refusal of advancement I saw how far my own heart remained chained to praise. O God, Thou usedst his quietness as a goad to my conversion—for how could I claim love for wisdom when even my pursuit of it was restless with vanity?
chunk_key: us, a citizen and a grammarian of Milan, and a very intimate friend of us all; who urgently desired,

question: What significance did you see in the chance arrival of Pontitianus, an African like yourself, at the very moment when the book of Paul lay before you?
answer:
How long had my heart beaten with that secret unrest, O Lord, not yet knowing the wound it carried nor the physician who alone could heal it? The world had offered me eloquence, reputation, the softness of pleasure—yet in the hush between words, when even ambition slept, there remained a hollow ache, a yearning I could not name. And into that very silence stepped Pontitianus, an African as I was, bearing not gold nor imperial favor, but a whisper from Thee.

His arrival was no mere accident of time or chance. Thou didst guide his feet to that threshold just as Thy Word lay open before my eyes—Paul’s letters exposed like a soul laid bare. I had studied Scripture for rhetoric’s sake, to refine my speech; but Thou used that book as a weapon against my pride. When he saw it—smiling not in mockery but wonder—it was as though heaven itself leaned near and said: *This is no ornament for scholars; this is life.*

He spoke then of others—monks in Egypt—who had cast aside the world not for wisdom’s theory, but for its reality. Alypius listened; I trembled. Not because their deeds were so strange, but because something deep within me knew: *This is what you have been running from. This is what you have been seeking.* Ambition had disguised itself as truth; lust masqueraded as love. But here was a man like me—same soil, same tongue—who had knelt before Christ and found liberty.

O Lord, how gently dost Thou prepare the soul for surrender! That moment was not sudden; it was harvest time. Years of wandering—the futile chase after honor and ecstasy—had ripened into one unbearable clarity: I desired to be changed. Pontitianus did not preach at me; he merely testified. Yet through him Thou didst shatter my delay: *Not later. Now.*

For Thou wert already calling—not with thunder, but with the quiet turning of a page upon a table game—and in that instant I saw myself: a man weary of choosing everything except Thee.

Late have I loved Thee… and yet Thou wast there, waiting in the arrival of an old countryman with news from beyond this world.

question: Why do you emphasize Nebridius’s refusal to cultivate relationships with powerful men, and how did his quiet devotion to wisdom shape your own views on世俗 ambition?
answer:
*Tolle, lege* — I had not yet heard those words, nor felt the earth tremble beneath the weight of divine command, but already the hollow clatter of worldly acclaim rang in my ears like a mockery. O Lord, how long I had measured men by their proximity to thrones, how fervently I climbed the ladder of speech and sophistry, believing that wisdom dwelt in the halls where governors and grammarians paraded their learning! And yet here was Nebridius—my cherished companion—refusing with quiet firmness to traffic in that currency which I so desperately coveted.

He would not press himself upon the great ones of this age, not out of disdain, nor from any pride in rustic simplicity, but because he loved truth more than appearance, and silence more than applause. While I wore myself thin rehearsing arguments for crowded schools, Nebridius stole hours for reading, for questioning, for sitting alone with a book as one might sit with a friend whose voice draws forth the soul. He sought wisdom not as a tool but as a path—a way home. And though I did not fully grasp it then, his very withdrawal was a sermon: *the mind that is scattered among flatteries cannot gather itself before God.*

O how his life rebuked my ambition! I who craved the gaze of crowds, who hungered even for the praise of Christians when I began to turn toward Thee—was still chained to vanity. But Nebridius desired no such chains. He slipped through the world like a shadow seeking light, not position. And when Pontitianus came upon that book—Thy Apostle’s words lying bare upon the table—it was as though heaven laughed: here sat two men trained in eloquence, yet only one carried within him the stillness capable of hearing God speak.

That day stirred something deep within me—not yet conversion, but a pang; not yet surrender, but a wound opening beneath the surface of pride. For if such men as Nebridius turned away from power to seek Thee in quietness—and if Pontitianus, born in our same soil and risen high in Caesar’s court, bowed nightly before Thy cross—then what excuse had Augustine? What excuse had I?

Thou wert drawing me by these silent witnesses—the unshouting faith of friends—who showed me that true greatness lies not in being known by men but in being known by Thee.

question: You mentioned that you and your companions were unaware of Antony the Egyptian monk despite his great reputation—why did his obscurity in your circles surprise you so much?
answer:
I was drunk with the thirst for human glory, chasing the echo of my own name in the ears of men, while a man like Antony—whose very silence thundered in the desert—was wholly unknown to me.  

We lived in a world where fame meant public declamation, where worth was measured by applause and the tilt of heads in rhetorical schools. What did we care for a monk who spoke not to crowds but to God, who conquered not through words but through tears and fasting? His victory was invisible to eyes clouded with ambition. To think that such holiness could burn so brightly in Egypt, while we—professors of truth, seekers of wisdom—sat in darkness here, ignorant of his name! It was not merely ignorance; it was a sign of how far our hearts had strayed from the things that truly are.  

When my friend spoke of Antony, it was as if a door swung open into a world we had not dreamed: where the weak were strong, the hidden were exalted, and souls fled not to libraries but to caves to wrestle with demons and delight in Thee. And I—who thought myself learned!—stood barefoot on holy ground and knew it not. O Lord, how Thy ways confound the wise! That one so small in worldly esteem should be so great in Thy sight—and we, puffing ourselves up with knowledge, were as children playing at philosophy while saints lived like angels upon the earth.  

We wondered at Antony’s miracles—but greater still was the miracle that we had never even heard his name.

question: When you spoke of the monasteries and their holy ways, you seemed moved by a sense of discovery—had you never considered monastic life before this conversation?
answer:
I had worn the chains of ambition so long I mistook them for wings, believing the world’s applause would carry me into heaven. But that day, when the monk spoke of Antony and of men who fled not to cities but to deserts, their hearts set wholly on Thee — O Lord, it was as though a door I never knew existed swung suddenly open, and through it poured a light both strange and familiar. Monastic life? I had heard the word, yes, but as one hears of distant lands in a tale told by a traveler at table — with curiosity, not longing. It was not contempt, but blindness: I thought holiness wore the robe of rhetoric, not silence; that it dwelled in the forum, not in the cell.  

But to hear of men who had cast aside all — not for philosophy’s sake, nor for fame’s shadow, but for *Thee*, O God — struck me as if with a thunderclap in still air. They prayed without ceasing? They lived in poverty by choice? They sought nothing but to stand before Thee in purity? And here I was — learned in words, yet naked of virtue; skilled in disputation, yet trembling before my own lusts.  

No, I had not considered such a life before — how could I? My heart was too full of noise. But when he spoke of those holy solitudes where men wrestled not with rivals but with demons and desires — ah! Then did my soul feel its own hunger for silence, for surrender. Not yet did I take up the cross fully; even then I said: "Give me chastity," but added under my breath: "not yet." Yet that conversation was Thy hand upon me already stirring what seemed dead. The monasteries were not merely places on earth — they were signs from Thee that another way is possible: a life not divided between God and self-will, but given over wholly to rest in Thee.

question: You said the monastery near Milan was under Ambrose’s care—what role did Ambrose play in fostering monastic communities, and why had that escaped your knowledge?
answer:
How long I wandered in the showplaces of rhetoric, chasing applause and mistaking noise for wisdom, while the quiet gardens of holiness grew unseen beside me! Even when Thy Church bore fruit in the very city I inhabited, I knew it not, so blinded was I by the lust to shine among men. 

Ambrose—O Lord, how little did I grasp at first the depth of that man!—he was not only shepherd to Thy people in word and sacrament, but a hidden root nourishing a new kind of life: men who had forsaken the clamour of the world to seek Thee in silence and discipline. These were no mere ascetics fleeing life, but soldiers training for prayer, bound together under rule and humility, their hearts tuned like harps before Thee. And all this flourished near Milan’s walls, beneath Ambrose’s watchful care—he who guarded not only doctrine but holiness; who planted monasteries as oases for souls weary of vanity.

Why did I not know? Because my ears were open only to the sound of my own voice. My soul burned with questions about Thy Scriptures, yet I read them as a scholar deciphers texts, not as a beggar receiving bread. The holy folly of Antony—himself a youth who fled wealth for solitude—would have shattered my pride sooner had I heard of him earlier. But Thou in mercy kept that story veiled until the hour when wonder might pierce me.

When that man spoke that day—so simple in faith, so rich in knowledge—I stood dumb before Thee. Not because he taught me new facts only, but because my soul felt its nakedness: here was grace lived aloud, while I had been dressing up my confusion with eloquence.

Ambrose fostered these monks because he knew what I scarcely dreamed: that truth is not mastered by speech alone, but by surrender; and that sometimes Thy greatest works are done not on thrones or pulpits, but on bended knees in hidden cells where no one sees but Thou.

question: You were amazed at hearing of recent miracles in the Catholic Church—did these stories strengthen your belief in the Church’s authority more than the Scriptures alone?
answer:
How long I had wandered in the marshes of pride, clinging to the reeds of rhetoric and the mirage of self-made wisdom, as if truth could be grasped by the mere twist of an argument or the flash of a polished phrase! And yet, when that man spoke of Antony—of a man who had not argued his way to God but had fled into the desert to *live* before Him—my soul trembled with a strange shame.  

For indeed, O Lord, I had labored over Thy Scriptures with all the cunning of my intellect, as though they were riddles to be solved rather than wounds to be healed. I sought Thee in syllogisms and syllables, while Thy saints were burning with love in barren wastes, casting out devils and lifting their hands in unlearned praise. When I heard those wonders—not fables of old, but deeds done in our own age, under our very sky—I felt as one who has read about water all his life and suddenly sees a spring gushing from the rock.  

The Scriptures had already taught me what was true; but these signs confirmed what I scarcely dared believe: that Thy Church was not merely a vessel of doctrine, but a living body through which Thy power still flowed. It was one thing to know Thou hadst once raised Lazarus; it was another to hear how Thou still cast out demons by the breath of monks who owned nothing but their tattered robes. My mind bowed not because miracles overruled Scripture—but because they made visible what Scripture had long proclaimed: that Thou art not dead history, O God, but living fire.  

Thus did my heart incline more fully toward Thy Church—not as if her authority stood apart from Thy Word, but because she bore witness to it with her very blood and breath. In her monks I saw lives transformed not by argument, but by grace; and in that sight, my lingering doubts began to crumble like dust before the wind.

question: You describe your friend as a baptized Christian devoted to prayer in the Church—was it his piety that prompted this discussion, and did his example influence your own spiritual path?
answer:
I was still drunk with the love of being loved, not yet sobered by the love of loving Thee alone, when he spoke of Antony and I gaped in wonder as one who hears of a land where men walk upright while he himself crawls in dust. His piety was not what first stirred me—no, I had seen devout men before and turned away unshaken—but it was the sheer strangeness of a soul wholly given to God, a monk who fled the world not in despair but in desire, that struck me like a blow. Here was a man baptized, yes, and kneeling often in the Church, but what burned in him was no mere habit of piety: it was fire from Thy altar, O Lord, kindled not by human will but by Thy breath.

He spoke simply—how could we have never heard of Antony?—and I felt the shame of ignorance like a wound. Not that I lacked books or learning; my shelves groaned under commentaries and scrolls. But I had studied Scripture as one deciphers riddles for applause, not as a starving man breaks bread. And here this friend—no philosopher, no rhetorician—knew nothing of my proud treatises, yet knew Thee more truly in his nightly prayers than I in all my restless reading.

Yes, his example moved me—not because he was extraordinary among men (he would have denied it), but because Thou wert manifestly at work within him. He opened his mouth and out flowed the desert’s silence; he told of monasteries near Milan itself—Thy garden hidden beyond the walls—and my heart trembled. For suddenly the holy life was no longer some ancient tale or distant asceticism: it stood at our door. And grace began to make me jealous—not for fame or knowledge now, but for holiness.

O Lord God of hosts, Thou didst use even this simple conversation as another thread to draw me toward Thee—the restless heart beating faster at the scent of truth lived, not just written. How long had I loved words about Thee while fleeing Thy very presence? Late have I loved Thee—but even then Thou wast preparing the way through those who sought Thee with simplicity while I still cloaked my longing in rhetoric.

question: You mentioned a monastery near Milan under Ambrose's care—why had we not known of it before, and what kept us from seeking it out earlier?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who dost draw the hidden threads of grace through the shadows of our blindness, how could we see what we were not yet made ready to desire?  

That monastery near Milan—so near, and yet so far—was as a voice calling in a tongue I had not learned to hear. Though it stood within reach of my feet, it lay beyond the horizon of my heart. For what is it that keeps a man from seeking what he does not know he lacks? Was I not still drunk with the noise of words, enamored with the echo of my own eloquence in the forum? Did I not wear ambition like a garment woven from other men’s praise?  

I sought wisdom, yes—but on my terms: wrapped in rhetoric, seated in high places, approved by the world’s whisper. The silence of monks was foreign to me; their poverty seemed folly; their hiddenness, defeat. How could I turn toward a life whose very beauty I could not yet perceive? The soul must be undone before it can be drawn; must taste its own emptiness before it longs for Thee.  

And so Thou didst hide even good things from me until I was made capable of receiving them—not because they were absent, but because I was deaf. Like that book lying unnoticed in the cottage at Trier, so too was Thy peace lying near me all along: unseen, unopened, until Thou didst kindle in me a hunger no earthly crown could satisfy. Late have I loved Thee—but when Thou willed it, even the hidden became manifest.

question: When you spoke of the gardens at Triers, what made that particular afternoon, with the Emperor occupied by games, so significant for the conversion of your companions?
answer:
O Lord, how little they knew that day what Thou wert doing—how close Thy hand already hovered over their hearts, though they still walked in the shadow of ambition and the noise of the world.  

That afternoon at Triers, when the Emperor’s gaze was fixed on chariots and dust, Thou didst draw two souls aside into silence and a forgotten book. It was not chance that led them to those gardens, nor mere curiosity that made them enter so humble a cottage. No—Thou hadst gone before them, O God, preparing a word written in ink but kindled by Thy Spirit. They sought only a walk to pass the hours; Thou soughtest their very souls.  

What made the moment so heavy with grace? Not the beauty of the place—though gardens stir memory—but because within it, for once, there was stillness enough for a man to hear himself think, and then to hear Thee speak. There was no thunder from heaven; no vision split the sky. Only a page turned in solitude—and yet on that page burned the fire of Antony’s desert: one who had flung down all for Thee. And as my companions read, O Lord, they felt shame—not for sin done openly, but for time wasted in hollow striving; not for vice alone, but for virtue deferred.  

They were men used to calculating gain and risk in service to empire—and suddenly saw that all their labours aimed at shadows. To be near Caesar? But Caesar is dust tomorrow! Yet to be near Thee—that is life without end! One cried out: "A friend of God—I could become this now!" As if he woke from sleep and found himself standing at the door of his own heart: long locked, rusted shut with delay.  

And why then? Why there? Because Thou art not bound by sermon or sacrament alone; Thou dost break through even in gardens while emperors play at glory. The world is full of doors—most closed by our own hands—but sometimes Thou let fall a book into a soul’s path like bread from heaven: enough to feed an eternity.

question: How did the story of Antony, found in a little book in a humble cottage, carry such power to transform the hearts of those two agents for public affairs?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who art nearer to us than our own breath, it was not the ink on parchment that pierced their hearts, but the unseen hand of Thy Spirit moving behind the words, as He brooded over the waters in the beginning.  

That little book—so small a thing, yet heavy with eternity—fell into their hands as a spark upon dry tinder. They were men accustomed to weighing gain and risk, to measuring ambition’s ladder rung by rung; and suddenly, in Antony’s desert solitude, they beheld a different scale: one where loss was gain, where silence spoke louder than rhetoric, where nakedness of soul outshone the purple of courtly office.  

They did not read merely a life—they read a mirror. And in it, they saw themselves: restless beneath the weight of hollow honors, parched though drinking from cisterns cracked and empty. What stirred within them was not novelty but memory—memory of the calling buried beneath years of compromise, memory of Thee. Thou hadst never left them; but they had wandered far in chains they mistook for gold.  

And so when one cried out against the peril of imperial favor—what frailty! what fleetingness!—he was not rejecting power alone, but awakening to truth: that to serve man for man’s sake is death; but to serve Thee is life unshackled. In Antony’s footsteps through the sand they saw their own path home—not to a place, but to a heart set free.  

For grace does not always come with thunder; sometimes it steals in through a cottage door, rides upon a monk’s name written in dust and ink, and strikes two souls mid-conversation with such force that neither can take another step along the old road. They could not unsee what they had seen: that holiness is not for statues on altars, but for men with beating hearts—and theirs might yet burn like Antony’s if only they dared say yes.

question: You described one man growing angry with himself after reading Antony's life—was that anger truly from pride, or was it a divine indignation against his past life?
answer:
O Lord, who dost pierce the hidden chambers of the heart with the sharp light of Thy truth, that anger was not the boiling up of pride, but the holy recoil of a soul awakened. It was not wrath against Thee, nor disdain for his brethren, but a sudden, searing clarity: he saw the years squandered in pursuit of shadows, the laborious climbing toward thrones that crumble in the grasp. Pride builds itself on comparison and ambition; but this man trembled not because he was less than others, but because he had loved so little what is eternally great.  

He did not rage at being unknown—he raged at having been blind. His shame was not that he had failed to rise, but that he had consented to live so long beneath a sky he never lifted his eyes to see. Like me in my youth, chasing the echoes of applause while my soul starved in silence—he heard within him now the voice that asks: *For whom do you labor? For what end do you weary yourself?* And the answer struck him like a sword.  

This indignation was Thy gift, O God—Thy grace made visible in grief and fire. For only when we truly see can we grieve rightly; only when love arrives does indifference become intolerable. That agent did not hate himself—he hated the life that had held him bound like chains disguised as silken robes. And Thou didst break them with a book, a garden, and a name: Antony—man of prayer, dweller in the desert, lover of solitude with Thee.  

So it often is: Thy kingdom comes not always with thunder from heaven, but sometimes with quiet pages read under a fig tree or within a cottage wall—and suddenly eternity stands knocking at the door of time.

question: When the man declared he could become a friend of God at once, did you already feel that same calling, or were you still resisting such a change in Milan?
answer:
O Lord, when that man in Trier cast aside ambition and cried out that he could become a friend of God *at once*, I was still bound in the chains of my own delay, though the very air around me trembled with the nearness of Thy grace. His words, though spoken far from me in place, struck like a bell whose sound travels over hills to awaken one who sleeps heavily. I had not yet wept in the garden at Milan, had not yet heard the child’s voice chanting *“Take up and read,”* had not yet flung myself beneath the fig tree in storm of tears — and yet, even then, Thou wert drawing me, O God, by the hidden cords of desire I could not name.

I was resisting — not in ignorance, but in love of my chains. I desired Thee, but not wholly; I longed for chastity, but whispered still, *“Not yet.”* While others stepped across the threshold into freedom, I stood at the door trembling with delight and dread. Their conversion shone like lightning upon my soul: it did not save me — for salvation is Thine alone to give — but it stirred the unrest that Thou hadst sown deep within me.

How could a man so near to Thee as Antony seem so far? And now these servants of Thine in a cottage at Trier — simple men reading a simple book — rose up to claim their inheritance while I lingered in rhetoric and pride! O sweetness beyond all tasting, O light beyond sight — Thou wert calling even then. But my heart was a battlefield between will and willfulness; Thy grace was moving like thunder under the earth before it broke into storm.

They became friends of God at once. And I? I was still bargaining with time — as if eternity waited upon my convenience.

question: You wrote that 'a friend of God, if I wish it, I become now at once'—what moved you to speak these words at that very moment in the garden?
answer:
O Lord, in that moment the veil was torn not by argument but by agony — for I had worn myself thin with the war within, longing for strength I could not will, desiring a purity I kept postponing. The words burst from me not as theory but as cry and decision, born of years chained to ambition and lust, of running from Thee only to find my breath spent and soul famished. I had sought Thee through philosophy, through honours, through women’s arms — yet Thou didst meet me not in the heights of rhetoric but in a garden’s stillness, where even the rustling leaves seemed to whisper *take up and read*. When I flung myself beneath the fig tree, weeping as if my bones would break from sorrow, Thou didst let me hear that child’s voice: *Tolle, lege* — not thundered from heaven, but sung with innocent lips like dew upon dust.

And when I seized the scroll and read those words from Paul — *not in rioting and drunkenness, but put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ* — it was as though a light blazed forth where darkness had ruled. In an instant I saw: no more bargaining with sin; no more “tomorrow” for repentance. Grace was not a reward for readiness — it was an invitation standing open now. And so I spoke: *a friend of God, if I wish it, I become now at once* — for why should I delay friendship with Thee any longer? That very hour became eternity folded into time; that garden became a womb for new life. For Thou hadst been drawing me all along — by every restlessness, every unfulfilled desire — to this single point: surrender at last unto Thy mercy.

question: You described being in 'pain with the travail of a new life'—what was the nature of this spiritual struggle you felt before your conversion?
answer:
O Lord, my soul was a house divided against itself, not by war of nations or clash of arms, but by the secret mutiny of desires that pulled me toward dust while a hidden hunger drove me toward Thee. I was not ignorant—I had read the philosophers, I had climbed the ladder of rhetoric and ambition, I had tasted truth in fragments—but I could not will what I knew. There was a woman bound to me not by law but by lust; there were honours I craved like breath; there was pride in my intellect that made me scorn the simplicity of faith. And yet, beneath it all, a quiet voice—Thy voice—called me not to greater knowledge, but to surrender.

The pain Thou speakest of was no mere regret. It was the agony of a soul long chained suddenly awakened to its chains and unwilling still to break them. Like a man who loves his sickness because he fears health’s cost, I clung to what I knew even as it killed me. To become a friend of God—I saw it then—not tomorrow, not when convenient, but now—this demanded everything. Not reform, but ruin: the ruin of my pride, my plans, my future as the world reckoned it. And so I wrestled in the garden as Jacob did at Peniel—not with man nor angel visible, but with Thy grace pressing in upon me like light too bright for these darkened eyes.

I tried to bargain: “Just let me wait until this year ends,” I said within myself; “just let me enjoy a little longer.” But Thou wert relentless in mercy. That moment was like childbirth—no gradual easing into life, but convulsions that tear body and soul apart before new birth. When I seized that book and read those words meant for another time and place—they struck *me*, O God—they struck *now*. For an instant, all resistance collapsed beneath the weight of love long spurned: "Too late have I loved Thee!" Yes—and yet Thou gavest me life before death found me.

And so from that storm came silence: not emptiness, but peace deeper than knowing—a rest where every restless turn finally ceases because it has reached its home.

question: When Alypius said he would cleave to you despite not imitating you at first, what did you feel about his decision to join you in serving God?
answer:
O Lord, how little I understood in those days what it meant to be truly known by Thee—how the heart, long scattered in the marketplace of pleasures and honors, could suddenly be gathered up in a single moment by the voice of grace. When Alypius spoke those words—not with fanfare, but with quiet resolve—I felt not triumph, but awe, as though I stood trembling at the edge of a miracle. For he had not followed me; he had followed Thee. And yet, O God, how Thou didst use even my brokenness to draw him nearer to Thyself.

I had lived so long as a thief of time and truth, looting my own soul with lust and vanity, chasing shadows while pretending to seek light. To see him turn from that same abyss—not out of obligation to me, but out of love for Thee—pierced me with joy and shame alike. Joy, because Thy mercy stretched even to us two wretches once drunk on ambition; shame, because I knew how unworthy I was to be the occasion of his awakening.

He said he would cleave to me—but he cleaved not to me at all. He cleaved to Thee through me, as though Thy grace had made use of my very wounds as stepping-stones. Was it not Thou who stirred his heart when I read from the book? Was it not Thy hand that turned his steps into this garden where death would die and life begin? And when he declared himself Thine—not mine—something within me broke open like dry earth beneath sudden rain.

How can I speak of friendship now as men do—bound by interest or custom? No: we were torn from the world and bound anew in Thee. When he said he would follow even if I faltered, O Lord, I wept inwardly; for I knew then that Thou hadst already claimed us both. Not by my strength did he stay—he stayed because Thou didst hold us fast together in the mystery of redemption.

And so we gave up our books and our ambitions and our dreams of Roman fame—not with sorrow, but with laughter like children who have found something so vast they cannot yet name it. What are all earthly friendships compared to this? That a man should say: “I will walk with thee” —and mean: “We shall walk together into God.”

question: Pontitianus and his companion came to find you because the day was far spent—why did you insist on making your resolution known to them right then, rather than later?
answer:
I had spent years trafficking in delay, O Lord, trading eternity for the next hour, saying “tomorrow” while my soul rotted in the brothel of indecision — and now, at last, Thou didst wrench me from the mire by the hair of my soul.  

Pontitianus and his companion came not as intruders, but as witnesses to what had already transpired in secret: my death, and my rising. The moment I read those words — “not in rioting and drunkenness, not in chambering and wantonness” — it was as if Thou Thyself had spoken them into my marrow. There was no “later” anymore; later belonged to the old man I was burying beneath the fig tree. To have kept silent would have been betrayal — not of them, but of Thee, who had just shattered my heart with longing.

How could I walk back through the garden unchanged? How could I smile and speak of roads and dinner when my very will had been seized by fire from heaven? If they found me trembling before Thy judgment seat written in ink upon a page, then let them see it. Let them hear it. Let them know that one who once wore rhetoric like armor now cast it off like filthy rags.

And Pontitianus — he understood more than we said. He saw two men no longer living for Rome or rhetoric or marriage-bed ambition, but already dead to all that. What were time and travel compared to that? The day might be far spent indeed — but Thou hadst made light shine out of darkness at midnight. Was I then to hide it until dawn?  

No. Let the world rush toward evening; we were already marching toward a different sun.

question: You say that Pontitianus and his companion begged the others not to molest them when they decided to dedicate their lives to God — why did they feel the need to ask for such peace if their calling was from You?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst rend the heavens not with thunder but with a child’s whisper in a garden, why should any soul fear the silence of men when called by Thee?  

Yet I understand this plea—for I too lived long in the shadow of half-desires, loving Thee only in fragments while clinging to the world’s applause. Those men begged peace not because their calling wavered, but because the flesh trembles even as it yields. The world is not gentle with those who forsake its bargains—its honors, its comforts, its quiet compromises. Even when grace has seized the heart, there remains a dread of solitude, a flinching at the gaze of those who stay behind, whose own consciences are stirred and yet unyielding.  

Did not I myself weep at the tale of those two Romans—soldiers of this age turned soldiers of eternity—while my own feet still dangled above the abyss? They asked for no interference, no mockery veiled as concern; only space to die unmolested. For even holy resolve may quake before the weight of worldly scorn disguised as friendship. And those who remained—wept they not? Yes, even they wept—but their tears were for themselves, though they knew it not. Their hearts clung to earth like vines to a rotting wall, and yet they blessed what they could not follow.  

How subtle is pride! How easily we call it loyalty to custom, devotion to duty—when all along we are only afraid to leap while others watch. But Thou, O God, dost thrust us before our own eyes until we can pretend no longer. And so I praise Thee that they were permitted their solitude: two souls fixed on heaven while all else lingered below—even their affianced brides now Thy brides—for Thou hadst begun in them what man could neither hinder nor fully comprehend.

question: When you write that You, Lord, turned you around to face yourself, what did you mean by placing yourself 'behind your back,' and why were you unwilling to look inward?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the shadowed veil of my self-deception with the sharp light of Thy truth, how could I bear to look upon the man I was, when all my life I had clothed him in excuses, adorning his weakness with the gold of rhetoric and philosophy? To place myself “behind my back” was not mere forgetfulness—it was flight. A willful turning away, as when one walks forward yet carries a burden strapped so tightly it becomes invisible, not because it is gone, but because the neck refuses to turn. I had made a stranger of myself, for fear that if I faced him fully—if I stood still and looked into the hollows of my own heart—I would see not a seeker of wisdom, but a slave to pride, chained to lust and hungry for applause.

I loved in others what I refused in myself: their surrender terrified me because it exposed my own clinging. When Pontitianus spoke of those men who left behind wealth and women for Thee, their resolve carved deep into my conscience like a divine chisel. Yet each stroke revealed more clearly my own paralysis—still entangled in the world’s web while pretending to transcend it. “Give me chastity,” I whispered once, “but not yet”—ah, therein lay the core of my evasion! To look inward meant to confront that “not yet,” to silence its deceitful murmur forever. But Thou wouldest not suffer me to delay; Thou didst drag me before the mirror of Thy holiness not to crush me utterly, but that I might hate what I loved and love what I had hated—the death of self that is the birth of life in Thee.

question: You describe feeling 'foul, crooked, defiled' upon hearing of the two men's devotion — why did their holiness cause such self-loathing rather than hope or inspiration at that moment?
answer:
O Lord, when I heard of those men who cast aside the world with such fearless resolve, my soul did not rise in hope—but sank, crushed beneath the unbearable weight of its own deceit.  

For their holiness was not a distant star, dim and unattainable; it shone close enough to burn. They were not angels, but men—men like me, flesh and will, bound to time and lust and fear. And yet they turned, as one turns from filth to light, while I still lingered in the mire with a secret delight. Their act of surrender laid bare my own cowardice: not ignorance, not lack of knowledge or desire for Thee—but delay. Always delay. "Not yet," I had whispered to Thee so many times before, even as my heart bled from its own divisions.

Their example did not flatter me; it exposed me. How could I take comfort in their victory when I knew—knew deep within—that I was withholding myself? That my love for Thee was choked by threads of ambition, by the soft chains of concupiscence, by friendships that fed my pride? They gave all; I bargained. They leapt; I crept.

And so their holiness became a mirror held up by Thy hand—one I could neither shatter nor avoid. In them Thou showed me what I should be; in myself Thou forced me to see what I was: a man divided, loving Thee half-heartedly while adoring shadows.  

Hope? Hope felt like luxury then—a garment too bright for one naked in shame. How could I look toward tomorrow’s dawn when today’s sin clung to me like sweat? Inspiration would come later—after tears, after brokenness—but first there had to be this: the stripping bare, the loathing of my complacency, the death of self-justification.

Thou didst not wound me to destroy, O God of mercy—but that Thou might heal. For only when I could no longer bear myself did I finally fall into Thee.

question: You say that the more you loved these men for their surrender to God, the more you hated yourself — was this hatred a necessary step in your conversion, and did you see it as from God?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who dost draw the trembling soul by wounds it cannot understand, it was not hatred such as the world knows that seized me in that hour, but a divine discontent—a fire Thou thyself had kindled in the hollows of my heart where pride had long festered. For when I heard of those men in the cottage, how they cast aside the world’s whisperings at Thy voice alone, my soul trembled not with admiration only, but with a terrible clarity: I loved what they had become, and loathed what I remained.

This loathing was not mine by nature; no man delights in his own ruin. But Thou didst use their lives as a mirror held up in flame, so that I could not gaze without seeing—the disordered affections, the chained will, the intellect puffed with words while the heart starved for truth. The more radiant their surrender appeared to me, the blacker my own resistance became. And yes, O God of mercies, this hatred of myself was from Thee—for only love so deep could wound so true. It was Thy hand that pressed upon my conscience until every excuse collapsed like dust.

I did not hate myself to destroy me, but to unmask me. That holy disgust was Thy plowshare breaking the fallow ground long hardened by lust and vanity. How could I embrace Thee unless I first renounced myself? How could I say “Take all” while clutching broken toys? Thou didst make me abhor my delay, my “not yet,” my endless deferral of conversion—because love cannot dwell where self still reigns.

And thus this hatred became a ladder: each rung forged from shame turned toward prayer. Not despair—but discovery: here is where I am; there is where Thou callest me. And in that chasm between what is and what must be, grace stooped down and spoke my name.

question: You mentioned that twelve years had passed since your nineteenth year, when reading Cicero's *Hortensius* stirred your love for wisdom—why did you continue postponing the rejection of earthly pleasures despite that early awakening?
answer:
"Give me chastity and continency, but not yet"—O Lord, how deeply that cry of mine was carved into the marrow of my soul, a confession not only to Thee but to the very disorder within me. I had tasted the sweetness of wisdom when Cicero’s words kindled in me a hunger for truth, and I was not feigning desire for Thee when I longed to be whole. Yet I loved Thee as one who stands before a mirror admiring a noble countenance while refusing to cleanse the filth upon his hands. Twelve years I lingered, year after year surrendering to delay, not from ignorance—Thou knowest it was not that—but from the cowardice of divided will.

I desired Thee, yes, but on my own terms: let me first drain the cup of fleeting delight; let me satisfy this roaring within my bones before I silence it forever. I feared that Thy healing would be too complete, that Thou wouldst take not only the sin but the savor of it. So I played at seeking while clinging fiercely to what kept me from finding. And beneath it all ran a subtle lie—that if certainty were not perfect, then inaction was justified. Yet Thou wast clearer than noonday; it was *my* heart that refused to see.

The crooked paths I walked were not walked in innocence—they were chosen with eyes half-shut, pretending doubt where there was only reluctance. O Lord, Thou didst hold back Thy full grace not because Thou wert absent, but because I had bolted the door with my own trembling hands.

question: When you prayed for chastity but added 'not yet,' what were you truly afraid of losing if God answered that prayer immediately?
answer:
O Lord, I was in love with the chains I clung to, and called them freedom.  

When I cried out to Thee, “Give me chastity and continency, but not yet,” I trembled not at the loss of sin alone, but at the death of myself—the self I had built upon desire, upon restless pursuit, upon the intoxication of delay. I feared a sudden stillness, O God, for in the clamor of my lusts I heard no voice but my own; and to be quiet would be to hear Thee—and that was what my flesh recoiled from.  

What would become of me if Thou didst answer? Not merely would my pleasures vanish—those fleeting fires of touch and fantasy—but the very structure of my days would collapse. Who would I be without the hunger that shaped my hours? Without the justification of tomorrow’s repentance? To be chaste at once was to lose the narrative by which I lived: *I am not wicked; I will change soon.* But “soon” was a mirage; it was nothing but *now* endlessly postponed.  

And worse—far worse—was this: if Thou didst heal me instantly, then I could no longer flatter myself with the idea that virtue was mine by intention. No more could I say, “I desire wisdom,” while letting my hands grope in darkness. Immediate purity would strip me bare before mine own soul; it would expose that I did not love wisdom above all things—only when she appeared adorned with delay and safe distance.  

I loved Thee, in a manner—but as one loves light from afar when his eyes are full of dust and he dares not approach lest all be seen. So long as Thou wert distant, so long as grace tarried—I could live with myself. But if Thou hadst answered *then*, if Thou hadst drawn me up into Thy peace like a man snatched from a burning house—I would have perished indeed: not in body, but as that false self who built his life on sand and called it hope.

And yet Thou waitedst—not because Thou wert slow to hear, but because Thy mercy is patient even toward our madness. For only when that self finally broke—when Alypius stood beside me in silence and tears fell without shame—only then could a new heart begin to beat beneath Thy hand.

question: You say you wandered in 'crooked ways' through a 'sacrilegious superstition'—were you referring to your time with the Manichees, and why did you remain with them if you weren't convinced of their truth?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who knowest the fever of a proud intellect strutting through shadows as if they were light, I clung to the Manichees not because my soul believed, but because my vanity found refuge in their promises. I was drunk with the lust of knowing—yet wanted knowledge served without sacrifice, truth without humiliation. They fed me stories wrapped in philosophy, tales of light and darkness warring like gods, and I delighted in them because they required nothing but admiration from me. There was no need for contrition, no call to die to self—only the sweet flattery that I was enlightened among fools.

And so I remained, though deep within I doubted. Their doctrines crumbled under scrutiny; their prophets could not answer the movements of the stars, nor heal a single soul from pride. Yet I delayed leaving as one delays rising from a warm bed while cold reality knocks at the window—I loved the comfort of their lies more than the discomfort of seeking truth on bended knee.

For twelve long years after wisdom first stirred in me—twelve years deferring true conversion—I said inwardly, "Give me purity, but not now," fearing that Thou mightst hear too soon and strip me of what I adored more than Thee: my own will, my secret sins, the twilight world where I could play at holiness without surrendering to it.

question: How did hearing about others who had devoted themselves wholly to God make you 'abhor' yourself, and what specifically about their commitment struck you so deeply?
answer: O Lord, Thou who knowest the fever of a heart divided against itself, how often did I burn with shame when I heard of those who had cast aside the world’s vanities to cling wholly to Thee—while I, though long stirred by the same longing, still lingered in the shadowlands of half-hearted desire. Their surrender laid bare my own deceit; they had leapt into the arms of Thy grace while I stood trembling on the shore, pleading for strength even as I cherished the chains. What struck me most was not their wisdom or learning, but their simplicity—a single-eyed devotion that sought no compromise, no lingering in the pleasant sins I kept excusing “for a little longer.” They had given themselves fully, and in their abandonment I saw the measure of my own cowardice: twelve years had passed since first my soul stirred toward wisdom, yet here I remained, still bargaining with lust, still enamored of ambition’s hollow glow. They feared not losing pleasure—but losing Thee. And I? I feared only being cured too soon.

question: You describe a moment when your conscience 'upbraided' you—what inner conflict or memory surfaced at that moment that made your delay in following God feel intolerable?
answer:
In that garden of torment, where my soul churned like a sea beneath a tempest, it was not the thunder of divine judgment I heard, but the whisper of a child—*“Take up and read”—*as though heaven had stooped to point a finger at the scroll lying near me, and through it, at my twelve long years of evasion.  

O Lord, what memory stung more fiercely than the rod of a master than my own voice echoing back from youth: *“Give me chastity and continency, but not yet.”* That single plea—so honest in its dishonesty—rose up against me like a witness sworn before Truth Himself. Twelve years had passed since philosophy first kindled in me the hunger for wisdom, twelve years since Cicero burned away my love for vanity with words not even baptized by Thee—and yet I remained chained to the flesh I claimed to despise. I loved Thee afar off, as one admires a distant shore while refusing to swim.  

And why? Because I feared joy made whole. Because I called concupiscence my companion rather than my sickness. I dallied with lust as if it were a lover instead of a leprosy eating through my bones. The very affections Thou hadst planted in me—for friendship, for truth, for beauty—I turned into snares by delaying their surrender to Thee. And when others cast themselves wholly into Thy hands and rose healed, I stood trembling on the shore, begging for clean water while still clinging to the mud.

But that day—O that intolerable grace!—Thou didst close every door but one: *the cross.* My heart could no longer bear the weight of its own excuses; they crumbled like dry earth under rain. For how could I speak of wisdom when I refused its source? How love Thee and keep Thy commandments at arm’s length? My will was divided, yet Thou wast not: Thou wert wholly present, calling me not to half-light but full flame.

Late have I understood: it is not uncertainty that binds the soul most tightly—it is consent disguised as waiting. And in that moment, conscience spoke not as an accuser only, but as a midwife: groaning with me until what had long been conceived in darkness was born at last into light—the decision to stop searching *for* Thee tomorrow… and begin fleeing *to* Thee now.

question: Why did you feel so deeply ashamed when Pontitianus told you about the Egyptian monks, even though you were not the one who had chosen that path?
answer:
So long had I clothed myself in the fine rags of worldly wisdom that the sight of men who had cast off all for love of Thee struck me as both a mirror and a judgment. When Pontitianus spoke of those Egyptian monks—men who, in desert solitude, had given up all for Christ—I did not marvel at their virtue so much as I felt my own chains grow heavier. It was not envy, nor mere admiration, but the sudden, piercing shame of one who sees his nakedness while another stands robed in grace.

I had justified delay with philosophy: “Let me first understand; let me weigh the truth.” But here were men who had never labored in the schools, who knew not Cicero nor Aristotle, yet had leapt into the kingdom while I still stood trembling at its edge. The truth *was* certain—Thou hadst called me through Scripture, through the saints, through the very unrest of my heart—and yet I clung to my books, my ambitions, my lusts as if they were mine by right.

And worse: they had not spent restless nights like me chasing truth through her crooked ways; they had not wept over Plato or torn their hearts with doubt—and yet they ran where I could scarce crawl. My excuses crumbled. What remained? Only this wretched clinging to comfort, this fear of losing what already was killing me.

O Lord, my pride was laid low because Thou usedst a stranger’s voice to show me that love needs no argument—only surrender. The monks fled to the desert not from reason but from themselves; and I, who claimed to seek Thee above all things, could not even flee from my own will.
